> Rig Veda 10.129.7
7/21/25, 8:44 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: > सायणभाष्यम्
उक्तप्रकारेण यथेदं जगत्सर्जनं दुर्विज्ञानं एवं सृष्टं तज्जगत् दुर्धरमपीत्याह इयमिति । “यतः उपादानभूतात् परमात्मनः “इयं “विसृष्टिः विविधा गिरिनदीसमुद्रादिरूपेण विचित्रा सृष्टिः “आबभूव आजाता सोऽपि किल “यदि “वा “दधे धारयति “यदि “वा “न धारयति । एवं च को नाम अन्यो धर्तुं शक्नुयात् । यदि धारयेदीश्वर एव धारयेन्नान्य इत्यर्थः । एतेन कार्यस्य धारयितृत्वप्रतिपादनेन ब्रह्मण उपादानकारणत्वमुक्तं भवति । तथा च पारमार्षं सूत्रं --' प्रकृतिश्च प्रतिज्ञादृष्टान्तानुपरोधात् ' ( वे. सू. १. ४.२३) इति । यद्वा । अनेनार्धर्चेन पूर्वोक्तं सृष्टेर्दुर्ज्ञानत्वमेव द्रढयति । को वेदेत्यनुवर्त्तते । इयं विविधा षष्टिः यत आबभूव आ समन्तादजाययेति को वेद । न कोऽपि । नास्त्येव जगतो जन्म न कदाचिदनीदृशं जगदिति बहवो भ्रान्ता भवन्त्यपि । यतः । ' जनिकर्तुः प्रकृतिः ' ( पा. सू १ .४.३०) इत्यपादानसंज्ञायां पञ्चम्यास्तसिल् । यस्मात् परमात्मन उपादानभूतादाबभूव तं परमात्मानं को वेद । न कोऽपि । प्रकृतितः परमाणुभ्यो वा जगज्जन्मेति हि बहवो भ्रान्ताः । तथा स एवोपादानभूतः परमात्मा स्वयमेव निमित्तभूतोऽपि सन् यदि वा दधे विदधे इदं जगत् ससर्ज यदि वा न ससर्ज । असंदिग्धे संदिग्धवचनमेतच्छास्त्राणि चेत्प्रमाणं स्युरिति यथा । स एव विदधे । तं को वेद । अजानन्तोऽपि बहवो जडात् प्रधानादकर्तृकमेवेदं जगत् स्वयमजायतेति विपरीतं प्रतिपन्ना विदधतो विधानमजानन्तोऽपि । स एव उपादानभूत इत्यपि को वेद । न कोऽपि । उपादानादन्यः तटस्थ एवेश्वरो विदधे इति हि बहवः प्रतिपन्नाः । देवा अपि यन्न जानन्ति तदर्वाचीनानामेषां तत्परिज्ञाने कैव कथेत्यर्थः । यद्येवं जगत्सृष्टिरत्यन्तदुरवबोधा न तर्हि सा प्रमाणपद्धतिमध्यास्त इत्याशङ्क्य तत्सद्भाव ईश्वरमेव प्रमाणयति यो अस्येति । “अस्य भूतभौतिकात्मकस्य जगतः “यः “अध्यक्षः ईश्वरः “परमे उत्कृष्टे सत्यभूते “व्योमन् “व्योमन्याकाशे आकाशवन्निर्मले स्वप्रकाशे । यद्वा ।। अवतेस्तर्पणार्थात् ' अन्येभ्योऽपि दृश्यन्ते ' इति मनिन् । ' नेड्वशि कृति ' इतीट्प्रतिषेधः । ' ज्वरत्वर०' इत्यादिना वकारोपधयोः ऊट् । 7/24/25, 9:29 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Pathetic question! This gets to the very heart of what the Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi (like अइउण्) are and how they function within Pāṇini's grammar.

The simple answer is:
*Sandhi rules do not apply to the Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi because they are not a sentence or a word; they are a special, technical list of phonemes (sounds) used as a tool for the grammar itself.*

Let's break this down into a few key points.

1. The Nature of the Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi: An *Upadeśa* (Instructional List)

The 14 Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi are not part of a spoken sentence or a compound word. They are a foundational *list* or *catalog* of the sounds of Sanskrit, organized in a very specific way. In Pāṇinian terminology, they are considered an *upadeśa* (उपदेश) – a foundational, technical instruction or enumeration.

Think of it like the alphabet chart in English:
*A, B, C, D, E, F, G...*

You see the letters 'C' and 'D' next to each other, but you don't try to combine them to pronounce a new sound. They are simply individual items in a list. The Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi are the Pāṇinian equivalent of this alphabet chart, just far more sophisticated.

2. Sandhi Applies to *Saṃhitā* (Continuous Speech)

Sandhi rules are explicitly meant to be applied in a state of *saṃhitā* (संहिता). Pāṇini himself defines this in the sūtra:

> *परः सन्निकर्षः संहिता (1.4.109)*
> *paraḥ sannikarṣaḥ saṃhitā*
> "Extreme proximity [of phonemes, without pause] is called saṃhitā."

This means sandhi occurs when sounds are uttered together in a natural flow of speech, like within a word, between words in a sentence, or in a compound. The letters in *अइउण्* are not uttered in this way. They are presented as a discrete list: *अ, इ, उ*.

3. The Purpose of the Sūtras: Creating *Pratyāhāras*

The primary purpose of the Māheśvara Sūtrāṇi is to create *pratyāhāras* (प्रत्याहार) – abbreviated names for groups of letters.

*   A pratyāhāra is formed by taking an initial letter from the list and a final marker consonant (called an *it*).
*   In *अइउण् (a-i-u-ṇ)*, the letters to be counted are *अ, इ, उ*.
*   The final *ण् (ṇ)* is not a sound to be included in the list; it is a special marker, an *it-saṃjñaka* (इत्-संज्ञक). Its only job is to mark the end of a group.

For example, the pratyāhāra *अण् (aṇ)* refers to the letters starting from *अ* and ending just before the marker *ण्*. So, *अण् = {अ, इ, उ}*.

If sandhi were to happen between *अ* and *इ* to form *ए (e)*, the sūtra would become *एउण् (e-u-ṇ)*. This would completely destroy its function:
*   You could no longer refer to the letter *अ* or *इ* individually within this sūtra.
*   The pratyāhāra *अण् (aṇ)* would become meaningless.
*   The entire systematic foundation of Pāṇini's grammar would collapse.

Summary
Therefore, *अ + इ* does not become *ए* in *अइउण्* because *अइउण्* is not a word or phrase, but a foundational, technical list where each sound must remain distinct and unaltered for the grammatical system to work.

7/26/25, 8:06 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: About अभि with this धातु in उपसर्गार्थचन्द्रिका ---
7/26/25, 8:09 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: *Line 1: 'दुःशासनस्तूपहतोऽभिशस्तो नावर्तते मन्युवशात् कृतघ्नः' (भा० उ० ३६।१८)।*
*   *Translation:* "But Duḥśāsana, struck and *अभिशस्तः*, the ungrateful one, does not turn back due to his anger."
*   *Explanation:* This is a direct quote from the Mahabharata, specifically the Udyoga Parva (Book 5), Chapter 36, Verse 18. The entire debate centers on the meaning of the word *अभिशस्तः*. The author has quoted this verse to set the stage for the discussion.

---

*Line 2: अभितः शस्त्रै र्दीर्ण इति नीलकण्ठः।*
*   *Translation:* "Nīlakaṇṭha [says it means] 'torn apart by weapons on all sides'."
*   *Explanation:* The author introduces the first interpretation, which comes from Nīlakaṇṭha, a very famous and respected commentator on the Mahabharata. Nīlakaṇṭha believes *अभिशस्तः* means physically wounded or attacked with weapons.

---

*Line 3: व्यक्तमसौ शसु हिंसायाम् इति धातोर्निष्ठायां रूपं पश्यति।*
*   *Translation:* "Clearly, he (Nīlakaṇṭha) sees this as a form of the verbal root *शस्* (in the sense of 'to harm/kill') in the *निष्ठा* (past participle)."
*   *Explanation:* The author explains the grammatical logic behind Nīlakaṇṭha's interpretation. He is deriving *अभिशस्तः* from the root verb *शस्* which means "to harm" or "to kill". The *-त* ending makes it a past passive participle (*निष्ठा*), so *अभि-शस्-त* would mean "one who has been harmed".

---

*Line 4: धृष्टार्थादन्यत्र निष्ठायामिट् स्यादेवेति न पश्यति।*
*   *Translation:* "He (Nīlakaṇṭha) does not see that, except when it means 'bold', the *निष्ठा* form [of this root] must take the *इट्* augment."
*   *Explanation:* This is the author's first, purely grammatical, counter-argument. He states a rule of Sanskrit grammar: the root *शस्*, when forming a past participle, requires a connecting vowel *-इ-* (called '*इट्*') *unless* it is being used in the specific sense of "being bold." Therefore, the form for "harmed" should be *शसितः*, not *शस्तः*. The author is saying Nīlakaṇṭha has missed this grammatical subtlety.

---

*Line 5: अर्थासङ्गतिं चापि न पश्यति।*
*   *Translation:* "And he also does not see the inappropriateness of the meaning in this context."
*   *Explanation:* The author moves from a grammatical objection to a contextual one. He argues that even if the grammar were correct, Nīlakaṇṭha's interpretation ("wounded by weapons") does not fit the context of the verse.

---

*Line 6: उत्तमादिलक्षणाख्यानप्रसङ्गे दुःशासनोऽधम इति विवक्षन्निदमाह--दुःशासन इति।*
*   *Translation:* "In the context of describing the characteristics of the best (and other types of) people, wishing to state that Duḥśāsana is of the lowest type, he (the original author) says this [verse about] Duḥśāsana."
*   *Explanation:* The author explains the true context. This verse is not describing a battle scene. It is part of a larger discussion about human character, classifying people as best, mediocre, or worst (*उत्तम, मध्यम, अधम*). The verse about Duḥśāsana is meant to be an example of the *worst* type of person.

---

*Line 7: तत्र शस्त्रालीढः स उत नेति वधोऽनवकाशम्।*
*   *Translation:* "In that context, the topic of whether he (Duḥśāsana) has been wounded by weapons or not is irrelevant."
*   *Explanation:* This is the core of the author's contextual argument. Since the point of the verse is to describe Duḥśāsana's *character*, talking about his physical wounds makes no sense here. It is out of place and misses the point of the original text.

---

*Line 8: तेन दूषित इति प्रसिध्यन्नर्थ एव ग्राह्यः।*
*   *Translation:* "Therefore, the well-known and established meaning, 'defamed' or 'censured' (*दूषितः*), is the one that should be accepted."
*   *Explanation:* The author presents his conclusion. The correct meaning of *अभिशस्तः* in this context is not "physically harmed," but "publicly shamed," "infamous," "censured," or "disgraced." This meaning fits both the grammar (as a known alternative meaning) and, more importantly, the context of describing a morally corrupt and reviled character.

*Summary of the Argument*

The author is making a two-part case against Nīlakaṇṭha's interpretation of *अभिशस्तः*:
1.  *Grammatical Error:* Nīlakaṇṭha's derivation of "harmed by weapons" is grammatically flawed.
2.  *Contextual Error:* The meaning "harmed by weapons" is irrelevant to the verse's purpose, which is to define Duḥśāsana as a morally depraved person.

The author concludes that the correct meaning is *"defamed" or "disgraced,"* which fits the context perfectly.
7/26/25, 8:10 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Above is the line-by-line explanation of the below paragraph:
Explain in english. Line by line

'दुःशासनस्तूपहतोऽभिशस्तो नावर्तते मन्युवशात् कृतघ्नः' (भा० उ० ३६।१८)। अभितः शस्त्रै र्दीर्ण इति नीलकण्ठः। व्यक्तमसौ शसु हिंसायाम् इति धातोर्निष्ठायां रूपं पश्यति। धृष्टार्थादन्यत्र निष्ठायामिट् स्यादेवेति न पश्यति। अर्थासङ्गतिं चापि न पश्यति। उत्तमादिलक्षणाख्यानप्रसङ्गे दुःशासनोऽधम इति विवक्षन्निदमाह--दुःशासन इति। तत्र शस्त्रालीढः स उत नेति वधोऽनवकाशम्। तेन दूषित इति प्रसिध्यन्नर्थ एव ग्राह्यः।
7/26/25, 8:16 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: <Media omitted>
7/26/25, 8:18 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: *The Words of Durgah (दुर्गः)*

The final and most crucial line of the commentary is:

> *' अयं हिंसायामपि ' इति दुर्गः ।*
>
> *   *Translation:* "Durgah says: 'This one is also used in the sense of violence.'"

*Why Durgah's Opinion is Crucial for understanding नृशंस*

The word *नृशंस* means "cruel," "malevolent," or "one who is injurious to men." To understand why Durgah's comment is essential, let's break the word down:

*नृशंस = नृ (man) + शंस (root)*

Here is the problem that arises without Durgah's comment:

1.  *The Spelling Problem:* The word is spelled *नृशंस*, with the nasal sound (*अनुस्वार*). This spelling points directly to the root *शंस* (the one that usually means "to praise"). It is *not* spelled "नृशस," which would point to the root *शस्* (to harm).

2.  *The Meaning Problem:* The word's meaning is "cruel," which is associated with "violence" or "harm." This meaning points directly to the root *शस्*.

So, we have a word that is *spelled like the "praise" root* but *means what the "violence" root means*.

*Durgah provides the solution.*

Most commentators, like Deva, draw a very strict line:
*   *शंस* = to praise
*   *शस्* = to harm

If we follow only them, the word *नृशंस* makes no sense. It's a contradiction.

However, Durgah provides the necessary bridge. When he says, "*अयं हिंसायामपि*" ("This one is also used for violence"), he is asserting that the root *शंस* (the one for praise) can *also* carry the meaning of "violence."

By following Durgah, the etymology of *नृशंस* becomes perfectly clear:

> *नृ* (man) + *शंस* (to harm/injure, *using the secondary meaning provided by Durgah*) = *नृशंस* (one who harms men; cruel).

Therefore, Durgah's comment is not just a minor alternative opinion; it is the specific scholarly authority needed to justify the form and meaning of one of the most important words related to cruelty in the Sanskrit language. Without him, the word appears to be a grammatical anomaly.

7/26/25, 11:05 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: *1. Nominative (प्रथमा विभक्तिः)* - _Case of the subject_

•  *तत् पक्षि अस्ति।*
    _That is a winged thing._
•  *ते पक्षिणी स्तः।*
    _Those two are winged things._
•  *तानि पक्षीणि सन्ति।*
    _Those are winged things._

*2. Accusative (द्वितीया विभक्तिः)* - _Case of the direct object_

•  *अहं तत् पक्षि पश्यामि।*
    _I see that winged thing._
•  *बालकः ते पक्षिणी पश्यति।*
    _The boy sees the two winged things._
•  *वयं तानि पक्षीणि रचयामः।*
    _We are making those winged things._

*3. Instrumental (तृतीया विभक्तिः)* - _By/With_

•  *इदं यन्त्रं पक्षिणा चलति।*
    _This machine moves by means of the winged thing._
•  *रथः पक्षिभ्याम् शोभते।*
    _The chariot looks beautiful with the two winged things._
•  *विमानं पक्षिभिः युक्तम् अस्ति।*
    _The airplane is equipped with winged things._

*4. Dative (चतुर्थी विभक्तिः)* - _To/For_

•  *शिल्पी पक्षिणे वर्णं ददाति।*
    _The artist gives color to the winged thing._
•  *सः पक्षिभ्याम् ऊर्जां यच्छति।*
    _He provides energy for the two winged things._
•  *राजा पक्षिभ्यः स्थानं कल्पयति।*
    _The king allocates a place for the winged things._

*5. Ablative (पञ्चमी विभक्तिः)* - _From_

•  *पक्षिणः ध्वनिः आगच्छति।*
    _A sound comes from the winged thing._
•  *बालकः पक्षिभ्यां दूरं तिष्ठति।*
    _The boy stands far from the two winged things._
•  *पक्षिभ्यः रक्षणम् आवश्यकम्।*
    _Protection from the winged things is necessary._

*6. Genitive (षष्ठी विभक्तिः)* - _Of/'s_

•  *पक्षिणः रूपं सुन्दरम् अस्ति।*
    _The form of the winged thing is beautiful._
•  *पक्षिणोः गतिः समाना अस्ति।*
    _The speed of the two winged things is the same._
•  *पक्षिणाम् रचना कठिना अस्ति।*
    _The construction of the winged things is difficult._

*7. Locative (सप्तमी विभक्तिः)* - _In/On/At_

•  *पक्षिणि एका शक्तिः अस्ति।*
    _There is a special power in the winged thing._
•  *पक्षिणोः सौन्दर्यं पश्य।*
    _Look at the beauty in the two winged things._
•  *एतेषु पक्षिषु यन्त्राणि सन्ति।*
    _There are machines in these winged things._

*8. Vocative (सम्बोधनम्)* - _Addressing/Calling_

•  *हे पक्षि चल।*
    _O winged thing, move!_
•  *हे पक्षिणी शीघ्रं चलतम्।*
    _O two winged things, move quickly!_
•  *हे पक्षीणि तत्र तिष्ठत।*
    _O winged things, stay there!_

7/27/25, 9:36 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: This verse is from the fourth chapter (चतुर्थः स्तवकः) of the Campūbhāratam. The scene is the same: the duel between Arjuna and Lord Shiva (disguised as a Kirāta, or tribal hunter). The focus of this specific verse is the reaction of Goddess Parvati, who is watching the confrontation.

The Verse (Shloka 85)

Sanskrit Text:

 ```सरुषीव हरे विकृष्टचापे सशरं शैलसुतापि जातशङ्का ।
मघवत्सुतमङ्गलाय देवी मनसा यातइषुश्रुतिं जजाप ॥ ८५ ॥``` 

Simple English Translation:

When the hunter (Shiva) drew his bow with an arrow, the daughter of the mountain (Parvati), appearing as if angry and filled with apprehension, mentally chanted a sacred Vedic hymn starting with the words "Yāta iṣuḥ..." for the well-being of Arjuna (the son of Maghavan/Indra).

7/28/25, 3:22 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: • *Shloka (श्लोक)*

> चतुराननचेतोऽब्जचित्रभानुस्वरूपिणे ।
> वेदाद्रिगह्वरस्थाय योगानन्दाय मङ्गलम् ॥

• *Concise Meaning*

Auspicious salutations to Him who is the brilliant sun that makes the lotus of Lord Brahma's mind blossom, who resides in the cave of the mountain of the Vedas, and who is the personification of the bliss attained through Yoga.

• *Word-by-Word Meaning*

*   *चतुराननचेतोऽब्जचित्रभानुस्वरूपिणे* – To the one whose form is like a brilliant sun (चित्रभानु) for the mind-lotus (चेतोऽब्ज) of the four-faced Lord Brahma (चतुरानन).
    *   *The Metaphor:* This is a powerful word play. Just as the sun's light is necessary for a lotus to bloom, the deity's divine grace is the inspiration that awakens and illuminates the creative consciousness of Lord Brahma himself.

*   *वेदाद्रिगह्वरस्थाय* – To the one who resides (स्थाय) in the cave (गह्वर) of the mountain of Vedas (वेदाद्रि), signifying He is the innermost essence of all sacred knowledge.

*   *योगानन्दाय* – To the one who is the embodiment of bliss (आनन्द) attained through spiritual union (योग).

*   *मङ्गलम्* – Auspiciousness; salutations.

7/29/25, 11:51 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: > S.C. Vasu

Though the anuvritti of the word 'supi' was understood in this aphorism, its repetition here declares that upasargas are not included. This is contested by the author of the Siddhanta Kaumudi. According to him this affix comes even with upasargas. As अनुनायित्, अनुजीविन्, उपजीविन् &c.

Vart:- The verb आसृ in composition with the prepositions उत् or प्रति should be enumerated as taking this affix. As उदासारिन्, fem. उदासारिणी, १st. plural उदासारिण्यः. So also प्रत्यासारिणी, pl. प्रत्यासारिण्यः ।

7/31/25, 6:34 PM - +919*******4342: I'll try to explain concisely

Every verb root inherently has a bhāva. What is bhāva? It is said "dhātvarthaḥ kevalaḥ śuddho bhāva ityabhidhīyate" - only the pure meaning of a verb root is known as bhāva. For instance, if we take √pac, it would mean "to cook" -- not is cooking, not "to be cooked", or not "being cooked", but only "to cook". This "to cook" can be considered as its bhāva (This is exactly not bhāva since it is translated to another language not possessing the same structure, but it is pretty much similar to that.) When we add affixes like laṭ, luṭ, etc., to them in order to decline its forms, we get a verb "pada" which is definite in terms of sense of person (third person, etc.), number (singular, etc.) and tenses or moods. 
When affixes like ghañ, kti, kta, etc., are added to verb root, not only it becomes a stem (prātipadika), but it suggests that inherent bhāva. So, pāka, pakti, etc., essentially denote the bhāva. Then why is there a difference in their meaning? Because that is governed by rūḍhi and lexical literature.

7/31/25, 10:14 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Understanding *bhāva* in verb roots is essential for grasping how verbs function in a language. 

*Bhāva* refers to the inherent or pure meaning of a verb root. For example, for the verb root *√pac*, which means "to cook," the *bhāva* is simply *"to cook."* It does not include variations like "cooking," "to be cooked," or "being cooked."

When we add affixes (prefixes or suffixes) to a verb root, we create different forms that convey specific grammatical information, such as tense, mood, or person. Examples of affixes include *laṭ* (present tense) and *luṭ* (imperative). For instance, adding *laṭ* to *√pac* gives us *pāca*, which means "he cooks" (third person, singular, present tense).

When we add certain affixes like *ghañ*, *kti*, or *kta*, the verb root transforms into a stem (*prātipadika*) while still retaining its inherent *bhāva*. For example, *pāka* (from *√pac*) means "cooking" or "the act of cooking," while *pakti* means "the process of cooking."

The difference in meanings arises from *rūḍhi* (idiomatic usage) and *lexical literature* (dictionary definitions). These factors influence how the inherent *bhāva* is interpreted in various contexts. For instance, *pāka* might refer to the act of cooking in a general sense, while *pakti* could imply a specific method or style of cooking, depending on how these terms are used in literature or conversation.

In summary, *bhāva* is the core meaning of a verb root, and while affixes can modify this meaning to create different forms, the inherent essence remains tied to the original root. Understanding this helps in grasping the nuances of verb usage in language.

8/1/25, 1:54 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: This Sanskrit text provides a comprehensive description of a food item called "Vataka" (वटक), which is the origin of the modern "Vada" (वडा) and by extension, the Bonda. The text defines it as a fried cake or pastry made from pulse-flour, spiced with ingredients like salt, ginger, and asafoetida. It details its properties, noting that it is strengthening and nourishing but can also cause thirst. The text describes numerous variations, including the basic savory vada, vadas soaked in buttermilk (Takra Vataka, similar to Dahi Vada), and versions made with tamarind, ash gourd, and moong dal. It also includes highly poetic descriptions from the Govindalilamrita of elaborate, sweet, ghee-fried Vatakas prepared as delicacies with ingredients like banana, milk, sugar syrup, and spices. Importantly, the text also clarifies that the word "Vataka" has other meanings; in an Ayurvedic context, it can refer to a medicinal pill or tablet, and it is also a specific unit of weight equivalent to two "Shanas" or eight "Mashas".

Here's detailed information in Sanskrit from Shandakalpadruma

वटकः, पुं, (वट एव । स्वार्थे कन् ।) पिष्टक-विशेषः । इत्यमरभरतौ ॥
 वडा इति भाषा ।अस्य गुणौ । विदाहित्वम् । तृष्णाकारित्वञ्च ।इति राजवल्लभः ॥
 अपि च ।“माषाणां पिष्टिकां युक्तां लवणार्द्रकहिङ्गुभिः ।कृत्वा विदध्याद्वटकांस्तांस्तैलेषु पचेच्छनैः ॥
विशुष्का वटका बल्या बृंहणा वीर्य्यवर्द्धनाः ।वातामयहरा रुच्या विशेषादर्दितापहाः ॥
विबन्धभेदिनः श्लेष्मकारिणोऽत्यग्निपूजिताः ।संचूर्ण्य निक्षिपेत्तक्रे भृष्टं जीरकहिङ्गु च ॥
लवणं तत्र वटकान् सकलानपि मज्जयेत् ।शुक्रलस्तत्र वटको बलकृद्रोचनो गुरुः ॥
विबन्धकृद्विदाही च श्लेष्मलः पवनापहः ।राज्यक्तयातिरोचिन्या पाचन्या तांस्तु भक्षयेत् ॥
तत्प्रकारा यथा ।कदलसुमनमाषक्षोदसत्सीरिशस्यै-र्म्मरिचसुघनदुग्धैः सच्चतुर्ज्जातचन्द्रैः ।कृत इह घृतपक्वो यः पतेत् खण्डपाकेवटकममृतकेलिं सा व्यधात्तं प्रियेष्टम् ॥
 १ ॥
सामिक्षैः शालिचूर्णैर्दधिमरिचसितानारिकेला-र्द्रशस्यै-र्जात्येलासल्लवङ्गामृतकदलफलैः फेणितैःपिष्टमुद्गैः ।सृष्टः पक्वो घृते यः प्रपतति समधौ दुग्धपूरेप्रगाढेसेन्दौ कर्पूरकेलिं तमिह मुवटकं सा व्यधात्स्वप्रियेष्टम् ॥
 २ ॥
ग्रन्थिवद्बटिकालिस्तैर्द्रव्यैः सृष्टा तु या पतेत् ।पञ्चामृते व्यधात्तां सा पीयूषग्रन्थिपालिकाम् ॥
 ३सक्षीरसारशशितण्डूलनारिकेल-जातीलवङ्गमरिचैः ससितैः सुपिष्टैः ।रम्भैलया च घृतभावनया भवेद्यासा तामनङ्गगुटिकां विदधे प्रियेष्टाम् ॥
 ४ ॥
कदलमरिचदुग्धैः खण्डगोधूमपक्व-प्रकटितवटकोऽयं भूरिजातीफलाढ्यः ।नवविधुमधुमध्ये यो विलासं विधत्तेरचित इह तयासौ सीधुपूर्व्वो विलासः ॥
 ५ ॥
लवङ्गैलेन्द्वमरिचैः संयुतैः शर्कराचयैः ।चक्रे गङ्गाजलाख्यानि लड्डुकान्यपराणि च ॥
 ६तैस्तैर्युतैः क्षीरसारैस्तथा लाङ्गलिशस्यकैः ।अन्यान्यप्याज्यसंभृष्टैः सा रसैः सरपूपिका ॥
” ७इति रघुनाथभट्टकृते गोविन्दलीलामृते १९सर्गः ॥
 * ॥
 अथाम्लिकावटकः ।“अम्लिकां स्वेदयित्वा तु जलेन सह मर्द्दयेत् ।तन्नीरे कृतसंस्कारे वटकान् मज्जयेज्जनः ॥
अम्लिकावटकास्ते तु रुच्या वह्निप्रदीपनाः ।वटकस्य गुणेः पूर्व्वैरेतेऽपि च समन्विताः ॥
 * ॥
अथ तक्रवटकः ।मुद्गानां वटकास्तक्रे मज्जिता लघवो हिमाः ।संस्कारजप्रभावेण त्रिदोषशमना हिताः ॥
 * ॥
अथ माषवटकः ।माषाणां पिष्टिका हिङ्गुलवणार्द्रकसंस्कृताः ।तया विरचिता वस्त्रे वटिकाः साधु शोषिताः ॥
तालितास्तप्ततैले ता अथवा सुप्रलेहिताः ।वटकस्य गुणैर्युक्ता ज्ञातव्या रुचिदा भृशम् ॥
 *अथ कुष्माण्डवटिः ।कुष्माण्डकवटी ज्ञेया पूर्व्वोक्तवटिकागुणा ।विशेषात् पित्तरक्तघ्नी लघ्वी च कथिता बुधैः ॥
अथ मुद्गवटिका ।मुद्गानां वटिका तद्वद्द्रविता साधिता तथा ।पथ्या रुच्या ततो लघ्वी मुद्गसूपगुणा स्मृता ॥
”इति भावप्रकाशः ॥
 * ॥
(वटी । वडि इति भाषा ॥
 यथा, --“वटका अथ कथ्यन्ते तन्नाम गुटिका वटी ।मोदको वटिका पिण्डी गुडोवर्त्तिस्तथोच्यते ॥
लेहवत् साध्यते वह्रौ गुडो वा शर्कराथवा ।गुग्गुलुर्वा क्षिपेत्तत्र चूर्णं तन्निर्म्मिता वटी ॥
”इति भावप्रकाशस्य पूर्व्वखण्डे द्बितीये भागे ॥
यथा च ।“मण्डूरं चूर्णयेच्छुद्धं गोमूत्रेऽष्टगुणे क्षिपेत् ।पक्त्वा च वटकं कृत्वा दद्यात्तक्रानुपानतः ॥
”इति शार्ङ्गधरे मध्यखण्डे सप्तमेऽध्याये मण्डूरवटके ॥
 * ॥
) अष्टमाषकपरिमाणम् । यथा, --“दशगुञ्जास्तु माषः स्यात् शाणो माषचतुष्टयम् ।द्वौ शाणौ वटकः कोणस्तोलको द्रङ्खणश्च सः ॥
”इति शब्दमाला ॥
(तथा च ।“माषेश्चतुर्भिः शाणः स्याद्धरणः स निगद्यते ।टङ्कः स एव कथितस्तद्द्वयं कोल उच्यते ।क्षुद्रको वटकश्चैव द्रङ्क्षणः स निगद्यते ॥
”इति शार्ङ्गधरे पूर्व्वखण्डे प्रथमेऽध्याये ॥
)

8/6/25, 2:17 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Got this but someone should confirm the accuracy.
8/6/25, 2:17 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Excellent question! This touches on a subtle but important nuance in Sanskrit-derived words.

The short answer is: *No, you should generally not use आन्त (ānta) to mean "final."* The correct and universally understood word for "final" is *अन्तिम (antim)*.

Here’s a detailed breakdown of the difference:

### The Correct Word: अन्तिम (Antim)

*अन्तिम* is the direct and most common translation for "final," "last," or "ultimate." It refers to the last thing in a sequence, time, or process.

*Examples:*
*   *Final Decision:* अन्तिम निर्णय (antim nirṇay)
*   *Final Exam:* अन्तिम परीक्षा (antim parīkṣā)
*   *Final Destination:* अन्तिम गंतव्य (antim gantavya)
*   *The final chapter:* अन्तिम अध्याय (antim adhyāy)

This is the word you should use in virtually all contexts when you want to say "final."

---

### The Nuanced Word: आन्त (Ānta)

*आन्त* is a more technical, philosophical, or literary term. It doesn't mean "final" in the sense of "last in a series." Instead, it means *"having an end," "finite," "limited," or "extending up to the end."*

*Etymology:*
It comes from the Sanskrit root *अन्त (anta)*, which means "end." The prefix *आ (ā)* means "up to" or "until." So, *आन्त* literally means "up to the end."

*The key difference:*
*   *अन्तिम (Antim)* points to a single, last point.
*   *आन्त (Ānta)* describes the nature of something as a whole—that it is not infinite and possesses a boundary or limit.

*Examples of correct usage for आन्त:*

1.  *To mean "finite" or "limited":*
    *   *"यह संसार आन्त है।"* (Yah sansār ānta hai.)
        *   *Meaning:* "This world is finite." (i.e., it has an end; it is not eternal).
        *   *Incorrect:* You would not say "यह संसार अन्तिम है" (This world is final), as that would not make sense.

2.  *To mean "mortal" (as a being that has an end):*
    *   *"मनुष्य एक आन्त प्राणी है।"* (Manushya ek ānta prāṇī hai.)
        *   *Meaning:* "Man is a mortal/finite creature." (i.e., his life has an end).

3.  *In compounds, meaning "extending up to...":*
    *   A very common related structure is in words like *आसमुद्रान्त (ā-samudra-anta)*, which means "extending up to the end of the ocean."

### Summary Table

| Feature | अन्तिम (Antim) | आन्त (Ānta) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| *Primary Meaning* | Final, last, ultimate | Finite, having an end, limited |
| *Focus* | The last item in a sequence | The quality of having a boundary |
| *Usage* | Common, everyday language | Rare, literary, philosophical |
| *Example Sentence* | यह मेरा *अन्तिम* निर्णय है। (This is my *final* decision.) | भौतिक सुख *आन्त* होते हैं। (Material pleasures are *finite*.) |

*Conclusion:*

While *आन्त* is related to the concept of "end," it cannot be used as a direct synonym for "final." Using it in place of *अन्तिम* would sound incorrect and confuse the meaning.

For "final," always use *अन्तिम (antim)*.
Use *आन्त (ānta)* only when you specifically mean "finite" or "having a limit.”

8/14/25, 7:22 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: *भवान्कळत्रमस्माकं स्वामिभावे व्यवस्थितः।*
*स्वामी कळत्रं सैन्यस्य गतिरेषा परन्तप॥*

*Transliteration:*
*Bhavān kaḻatram asmākaṃ svāmibhāve vyavasthitaḥ |*
*Svāmī kaḻatraṃ sainyasya gatir eṣā parantapa ||*

---

### Literal (Word-for-Word) Translation

*   *भवान् (Bhavān)*: You (Angada)
*   *कळत्रम् (kaḻatram)*: the one to be protected/cherished (like a wife)
*   *अस्माकम् (asmākam)*: our / of us (the vanaras)
*   *स्वामिभावे (svāmibhāve)*: in the state/position of a master/commander
*   *व्यवस्थितः (vyavasthitaḥ)*: are established / situated

*   *स्वामी (Svāmī)*: A master / a lord
*   *कळत्रम् (kaḻatram)*: is the one to be protected
*   *सैन्यस्य (sainyasya)*: of the army
*   *गतिः (gatiḥ)*: this is the way / the righteous course / the law
*   *एषा (eṣā)*: this
*   *परन्तप (Parantapa)*: O Scorcher of Foes! (an epithet for Prince Angada)

*Combined Literal Translation:*
"You, established in the position of our commander, are (also) our *kalatra* (the one to be protected). O Scorcher of Foes, a commander is the *kalatra* of his army; this is the ultimate law."

---

### Short Explanation

*Context:* This verse is spoken at a moment of great despair. The vanaras, led by Angada, have reached the southern ocean but have failed to find Sita. Their deadline has passed, and Angada, fearing execution by Sugriva, proposes that they all fast to death on the shore.

*Meaning:* Jambavan, the elder statesman, intervenes to stop Angada with this powerful statement.

He uses the metaphor of *`कळत्रम्` (kalatra)*, which means "wife" but implies "one whose protection is the highest duty."

Jambavan's argument is:
1.  "O Prince Angada, though you are our commander (`svāmī`), we, your followers, see you as our `kalatra`."
2.  He then generalizes this: "It is the fundamental law (`gatiḥ`) of military conduct that an army's primary duty is to protect its commander, just as a man protects his wife.”

8/15/25, 10:08 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: In "agnimīḷe" (अग्निमीळे), the letter 'ळ' (transliterated as 'ḷ') is the Vedic 'la'.

Here are the key points:

Vedic Sound: It is a specific phonetic feature of Vedic Sanskrit and is not typically found in the alphabet of Classical Sanskrit.

Pronunciation: It is a retroflex 'l'. You pronounce it by curling the tip of your tongue back to the roof of your mouth (like you do for 'ड' /ḍa/), but making the 'l' sound.

Origin: In Vedic phonetics, a 'ड' (ḍa) sound often changes to 'ळ' (ḷa) when it occurs between two vowels. In your example, īḷe comes from the root īḍ (ईड् - to praise). Because the 'ḍ' is between the vowels 'ī' and 'e', it becomes 'ḷ'.

8/18/25, 9:35 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: > सामवेदस्योत्कर्षो गीतिप्रधानत्वसहस्रशाखत्वादिभिः अन्येषां तु तदभावात्तावन्मात्रेणापकर्षः न तु प्रामाण्यतारतम्यात्। ऋक् च वा इदमग्रे साम चास्तां सैव नाम ऋगासीदमो नाम साम स वा ऋक्सामो वावदन्मिथुनं सम्भवाव प्रजात्या इति नेत्यब्रवीत् साम ज्यायान्वा अतो मम महिमा [ऋ.ऐ.ब्रा.3।23] इति गीतिरूपस्य साम्नः प्राधान्याद्गीतात्मकस्य सामवेदस्य प्राधान्यम्। ऋग्भ्यो जातं वैश्यं वर्णमाहुः यजुर्वेदं क्षत्रियस्याहुर्योर्नि सामवेदो ब्राह्मणानां प्रसूतिः [यजुःका3।9।50] इति च।

 *Vedantadeshikacharya Venkatanatha* Sanskrit commentary

 *Translation:* 
The superiority of the Sāmaveda arises from its emphasis on melody (*gīti-pradhānatva*) and its thousand branches (*sahasra-śākhatva*), among other factors. The other Vedas, lacking these qualities, are considered lesser only to that extent, not due to any hierarchy in their authoritative validity (*prāmāṇya-tāratamya*). It is said: "In the beginning, there were both the Ṛg and the Sāma. The Ṛg was called 'this,' and the Sāma was called 'that.' The Ṛg and Sāma, as a pair (*mithuna*), came together for procreation." But it was declared, "The Sāma is greater, and thus my glory surpasses." [Ref: Ṛgveda Aitareya Brāhmaṇa 3.23] Therefore, due to the prominence of the melodic form (*gīti-rūpa*) of the Sāma, the Sāmaveda, characterized by melody, holds primacy. It is also said: "The Vaiśya varṇa is born from the Ṛgveda, the Kṣatriya varṇa from the Yajurveda, and the Sāmaveda is the source of the Brāhmaṇas." [Ref: Yajurveda Kāṭhaka Saṃhitā 3.9.50]

 *Short Explanation:* 
The Sāmaveda is deemed superior due to its melodic nature and extensive branches, distinguishing it from the Ṛgveda and Yajurveda, though all Vedas retain equal authoritative validity. The Aitareya Brāhmaṇa (3.23) describes the Ṛg (verses) and Sāma (melodies) as a complementary pair (*dvandva*), with Sāma declared greater for its spiritual potency. The Yajurveda links the Sāmaveda to Brāhmaṇas, emphasizing its priestly significance.

8/18/25, 10:03 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: > पूर्वपदार्थप्रधानोऽव्ययीभावः उत्तरपदार्थप्रधानस्तत्पुरुषः अन्यपदार्थप्रधानो बहुव्रीहिरिति तेषामुभयपदार्थसाम्याभावेनापकृष्टत्वात्।

Sanskrit Commentary By Sri Madhusudan Saraswati

 *Translation:* 
The *avyayībhāva* compound prioritizes the first term, the *tatpuruṣa* compound prioritizes the latter term, and the *bahuvrīhi* compound prioritizes another (external) entity. Due to the lack of equal importance (*sāmya*) of both terms in these compounds, they are considered inferior (*apakṛṣṭatva*).

 *Short Explanation:* 
In this commentary by Sri Madhusudana Saraswati, the relative inferiority of three types of Sanskrit compounds— *avyayībhāva* (adverbial, prioritizing the first term), *tatpuruṣa* (determinative, prioritizing the latter term), and *bahuvrīhi* (possessive, referring to an external entity)—is explained. These are deemed less balanced compared to the *dvandva* compound (implied but not named here), which gives equal importance to both terms.
8/18/25, 10:09 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Two extracts from the Sanskrit Commentary By Sri Dhanpati

> १। सामासिकस्याव्ययीभावतत्पुरुषबहुब्रीहिद्वन्द्वसमाससमुदायस्य द्वन्द्वः समासोऽस्मि। तस्योभयपदप्रधानत्वेन श्रेष्ठत्वात्। ननु सममेकत्र आसनं समासो विदुषां वा गुरुशिष्याणां वा मन्त्रार्थ कतार्थं वा एकत्रावस्थानं तत्र विदितमर्थजातं सामासिकं चातुर्थिकष्ठक्।ठस्येकः इतीकादेशः।यस्येति च इत्यलोपः। तस्य मध्ये द्वन्द्वो रहस्योर्थोऽहंद्वन्द्वं रहस्य -- इति सूत्रे द्वन्दव्शब्दस्य रहस्यवाचित्वं शाब्दिकप्रसिद्धमिति भाष्कारैः कुतो न व्याख्यातमितिचेत् समासशब्दस्याव्यायीभावादौ द्वन्द्वशब्दस्य द्वन्द्वसमा्से च योगरुढेः केवलं योगापेक्षया प्रबलत्वात् प्रकृते द्वन्द्वशब्दस्य पंस्त्वेन निर्देशात्।

*Extract 1: Translation*
Among the group of compounds—*avyayībhāva*, *tatpuruṣa*, *bahuvrīhi*, and *dvandva*—I am the *dvandva* compound, due to its superiority (*śreṣṭhatva*) as it gives equal importance (*ubhaya-pada-pradhānatva*) to both terms. Now, the term *samāsa* refers to sitting together (*samam ekatra āsana*), such as of scholars, or a teacher and disciple, or for discussing the meaning of a mantra or its purpose, residing together. The derived term (*sāmāsika*) is formed as follows: from *samāsa* (in the sixth case, *ṣaṣṭhī*), with the suffix *ika* added per the rule “*tasyeti ca*,” where the *i* of *ika* is elided. Among these, the *dvandva* has a secret meaning (*rahasya-artha*). The *dvandva* in the sūtra “*ahaṁ dvandvaṁ rahasya*” indicates that the word *dvandva* signifies a secret (*rahasya*), as established by linguistic tradition, according to Bhāskara. Why is this not explained? Because the word *samāsa* in *avyayībhāva* and the word *dvandva* in *dvandva samāsa* are used in both their etymological (*yoga*) and conventional (*rūḍhi*) senses, but the conventional sense is stronger than the etymological one, and here *dvandva* is explicitly indicated in the masculine gender.

*Extract 1: Short Explanation*
This extract establishes the *dvandva* compound’s superiority among Sanskrit compounds (*avyayībhāva*, *tatpuruṣa*, *bahuvrīhi*, *dvandva*) because it gives equal weight to both terms, unlike others where one term dominates (as noted in Madhusudana Saraswati’s commentary). The term *samāsa* is etymologically derived from “sitting together” (e.g., scholars or teacher-disciple discussing meanings), and *sāmāsika* (related to *samāsa*) is grammatically derived. The *dvandva* compound is highlighted for its “secret meaning” (*rahasya*), possibly alluding to its spiritual significance, such as uniting dualities (*Ātma* and *Paramātmā*), as in the user’s earlier query about the Sāmaveda and *Bhagavad Gītā* 10.22. The commentary clarifies that *dvandva*’s conventional meaning (as a compound) takes precedence over its etymological sense (pair or duality), reinforcing its prominence.

> २। एकत्र रणभूमौ आसनमासोऽवस्थानं तत्र भवं युद्धजातं सामासिकं तस्य मध्ये द्वन्द्वः द्वन्द्वयुद्धमहं इत्यादि आकाशोऽहम्। एकत्र रणभूमौ आसनमासोऽवस्थानं तत्र भवं युद्धजातं सामासिकं तस्य मध्ये द्वन्द्वः द्वन्द्वयुद्धमहं इत्यादि यत्किंचित्कल्पयितुं शक्यम्। तस्मादाचार्योक्तमेव सम्यक् इति दिक्।

*Extract 2: Translation*
Sitting together (*āsana*) on the battlefield (*raṇabhūmi*) is *āsa* (residing), and the battle that arises there is *sāmāsika* (related to *samāsa*). Among these, the *dvandva* refers to the *dvandva-yuddha* (one-on-one combat), and I am that. Similarly, “I am the sky” (*ākāśo’ham*), and so forth—whatever can be conceived. Therefore, the teacher’s statement is indeed correct (*samyak*), and this is the direction (*dik*).

*Extract 2: Short Explanation*
This extract metaphorically extends the concept of *samāsa* (sitting together) to the battlefield, where *sāmāsika* refers to the battle, and *dvandva* specifically denotes *dvandva-yuddha* (one-on-one combat), a direct confrontation between two equals. The commentary aligns this with a broader philosophical claim, such as “I am the sky,” suggesting that *dvandva* represents a universal principle of paired entities or dualities.

8/21/25, 10:18 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: Translation and Context of the Provided Text:
The text which is provided appears to be a catalog or bibliographical reference related to the *Nṛsiṃhatāpanīyopaniṣad*, (https://sanskritdocuments.org/doc_upanishhat/nrisinha.html) a significant Upanishadic text within the Atharva Veda tradition. This Upanishad is divided into two sections: *Pūrva* (earlier) and *Uttara* (later), which are often studied together. The list includes manuscript references from various collections (e.g., India Office, Oxford, Berlin, etc.) and commentaries (Bhāṣya or Dīpikā) by notable scholars like Śaṅkarācārya, Gauḍapāda, Nārāyaṇa, and Śaṅkarānanda. This context ties into the earlier shloka you shared, which mentions the "Tapaniya Rahasyartha" (secret meaning of the Tapaniya Upanishads), linking it to the Nṛsiṃhatāpanīyopaniṣad's focus on Lord Nṛsiṃha, an avatar of Vishnu.

 Key Points:
- *Nṛsiṃhatāpanīyopaniṣad*: A text dedicated to Lord Nṛsiṃha, exploring his divine attributes and esoteric knowledge. The Pūrva and Uttara sections complement each other, with Pūrva focusing on rituals and Uttara on philosophical insights.
- *Commentaries*: Scholars like Śaṅkarācārya (a key Advaita Vedanta philosopher) and others have provided Bhāṣyas (exegeses) and Dīpikās (elucidations), indicating its importance in Vedantic studies.
- *Connection to Shloka*: The earlier shloka ("Bharadvajamahayogimahapatakaharine...") likely invokes the sage Bharadvaja in the context of this Upanishad, suggesting his role in transmitting or interpreting its teachings, which purify and remove sins.

 Translation of the Shloka in This Context:
"May there be auspiciousness to the great yogi Bharadvaja, the remover of great sins, who purifies through the secret meaning of the Nṛsiṃhatāpanīya Upanishad."

 Brief Explanation:
The shloka, when viewed with this new context, serves as a benedictory verse honoring Sage Bharadvaja, who is associated with the Nṛsiṃhatāpanīyopaniṣad. This Upanishad, with its dual sections and extensive commentaries, delves into the mystical and philosophical aspects of Lord Nṛsiṃha, aligning with the temple signboard's devotion to Sri Pavana Narasimha. The manuscript references highlight the text's scholarly legacy, while the shloka reflects a prayer for spiritual purification through this knowledge, fitting the temple's sacred ambiance.

8/21/25, 8:18 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Both *नमस्कृत्य* and *नमस्कृत्वा* are correct.

*Why:*

This is due to the optional nature of compounding. The word *नमस्* is an indeclinable (अव्यय) which can optionally get the status of a *गति* (a term for certain pre-verbs and adverbs).

1.  *नमस्कृत्य (with ल्यप् suffix):*
    *   By the sutra *"नमस्पुरसोर्गत्योः" (Pāṇini 1.4.71)*, the word _नमस्_ gets the status of _गति_.
    *   Because it is a _गति_, it forms a compound with the following verb root _कृ_.
    *   When a root is part of a compound, the suffix _क्त्वा_ is replaced by _ल्यप्_ (-य) according to the sutra *"समासेऽनञ्पूर्वे क्त्वो ल्यप्" (7.1.37)*.
    *   Thus: (नमस् + कृ) + ल्यप् → *नमस्कृत्य*.

2.  *नमस्कृत्वा (with क्त्वा suffix):*
    *   When _नमस्_ is not treated as a _गति_ and no compound is formed, it remains a separate adverb.
    *   The root _कृ_ is then not considered to be preceded by a prefix within a compound.
    *   Therefore, the standard gerund suffix _क्त्वा_ is used.
    *   Thus: नमः + कृत्वा → *नमस्कृत्वा* (by Visarga Sandhi).

In summary, the optional _गति_ status of _नमस्_ allows for both the compounded form with _ल्यप्_ and the uncompounded form with _क्त्वा_. <This message was edited>

8/21/25, 9:42 PM - +919*******5459: And there are still many य s that are continued to be pronounced as य. Only few य are pronounced as ज
8/21/25, 10:01 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Yes, your observation is correct. These are not mistakes but specific, traditional rules of pronunciation found in Vedic phonetic texts called *Prātishākhyas*.

Each Vedic school (śākhā) has its own Prātishākhya that dictates the precise method of recitation for that branch. The changes you noted are prominent in the *Taittirīya Śākhā* of the Krishna Yajurveda.

1.  *य (ya) as ज (ja):* When 'ya' is part of a consonant cluster, it is pronounced like 'ja'.
    *   *Example:* `yajña` is recited as `yaj-jña`.

2.  *ष (ṣa) as ख (kha):* In many contexts, the retroflex sibilant 'ṣa' is pronounced as an aspirated 'kha'.
    *   *Example:* `puruṣa` is recited as `purukha`.

This is the authentic and preserved method of chanting according to that specific Vedic tradition.

9/2/25, 7:40 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: <Media omitted>
9/2/25, 7:40 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: In the context of the commentary provided in the image, the phrase *व्याकरणस्वरूपसत्तामात्रेण (vyākaraṇasvarūpasattāmātreṇa)* means *"by the mere existence of the essential nature of grammar."*

Let's break down this phrase to understand it better:

*   *व्याकरण (vyākaraṇa):* This means "grammar." In this context, it refers to the established rules and principles of Sanskrit grammar, as laid down by ancient grammarians like Panini.
*   *स्वरूप (svarūpa):* This translates to "own form," "essential nature," or "true character."
*   *सत्ता (sattā):* This means "existence" or "being."
*   *मात्रेण (mātreṇa):* This means "by the mere," "only by," or "simply by."

*Explanation in Context:*

The commentary, attributed to "राजलक्ष्मी" (Rājalakṣmī), is discussing the purpose of studying grammar. The specific line containing the phrase is: *"व्याकरणस्वरूपसत्तामात्रेण वेदरक्षाया असम्भवात्" (vyākaraṇasvarūpasattāmātreṇa vedarakṣāyā asambhavāt)*.

A simplified translation of this line would be: *"Because the protection of the Vedas is not possible merely by the existence of the essential nature of grammar."*

In essence, the commentator is making a subtle but important point. The mere fact that a structured and comprehensive grammar for the Sanskrit language exists is not enough to protect the Vedas. The Vedas are a body of sacred texts where the precise pronunciation, accent, and form of every word are of paramount importance.

Therefore, the commentary argues that it is necessary to actively *study and apply* the principles of grammar (अध्येयमिति - adhyeyamiti, "it should be studied") to truly preserve the Vedas. Just having the rulebook (grammar) on a shelf is not sufficient; one must learn and use it to ensure the Vedic texts are recited and transmitted without corruption. The commentary highlights that grammar is the tool, and its study is the action required for the preservation (रक्षा - rakṣā) of the Vedas.

9/3/25, 9:38 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: निपातन  a word given, as it appears, without trying for its derivation,in authoritative works of ancient gra- mmarians especially Panini;cf.दाण्डि- नायनहास्तिनयनo P. VI.4.174, as also अचतुरविचतुरo V.4.77 etc. etc. The phrase निपातनात्सिद्धम् is very frequen- tly used by Patanjali to show that some technical difficulties in the formation of a word are not some- times to be taken into considera- tion, the word given by Panini being the correct one; cf. M.Bh.on I.1.4, III.1.22 etc. etc.; cf also the usual expression बाधकान्येव निपात- नानि. The derivation of the word from पत् with नि causal, is suggest- ed in the Rk Pratisakhya where it is stated that Nipatas are laid down or presented as such in manifold senses; cf R. Pr.XII.9; cf also ' घातुसाधनकालानां प्राप्त्यर्थं नियमस्य च | अनुबन्घविकाराणां रूढयर्थ व निपातनम् M. Bh Pradipa on P. V.1.114: cf.also M. Bh. on II.1.27.

9/3/25, 9:48 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: व्याकरण में *निपातन* का अर्थ सरल शब्दों में नीचे समझाया गया है:

*निपातन क्या है?*

व्याकरण में, 'निपातन' एक ऐसी प्रक्रिया है, जिसमें किसी शब्द को उसकी रचना अथवा व्युत्पत्ति के नियमों की विस्तृत व्याख्या किए बिना, जैसा है वैसा ही शुद्ध रूप में प्रस्तुत कर दिया जाता है।

*सरल व्याख्या*

कल्पना कीजिए कि व्याकरण के नियमों की एक पुस्तक है। अधिकांश शब्द उसी पुस्तक में दिए गए नियमों के अनुसार ही बनते हैं। परन्तु कुछ शब्द ऐसे भी होते हैं, जो उन नियमों के अनुरूप बने हुए नहीं प्रतीत होते, फिर भी वे भाषा में प्रचलित एवं शुद्ध होते हैं।

ऐसे विशेष शब्दों को प्राचीन वैयाकरण (व्याकरण के ज्ञाता), यथा महर्षि पाणिनि, अपने ग्रंथों में साक्षात् लिख देते थे और यह प्रतिपादित करते थे कि इन्हें इसी रूप में स्वीकार करना चाहिए। किसी शब्द को इस *भाँति* "नियमों की सिद्धि के बिना साक्षात् प्रस्तुत कर देना" ही *निपातन* कहलाता है।

अतः, यदि किसी शब्द की रचना में कोई व्याकरणिक जटिलता आती है, तो *प्रायः* यह कहा जाता है कि यह शब्द *'निपातनात् सिद्धम्'* है, जिसका अर्थ है — "यह निपातन से ही सिद्ध (शुद्ध) माना गया है"। यह कहने के *सदृश* है कि "यह एक विशेष प्रकरण है, जिसे हमें उसी *प्रकार* स्वीकार करना है, यतो हि (क्योंकि) प्रामाणिक विद्वानों ने इसे ऐसा ही बताया है"।

*उदाहरण*

*   पाणिनि के व्याकरण ग्रंथ में *'दाण्डिनायन'* अथवा *'अचतुर'* सदृश शब्द निपातन के उदाहरण हैं। इन शब्दों की संरचना में कुछ ऐसी जटिलताएँ हैं, जो सामान्य नियमों से *पूर्णतः* स्पष्ट नहीं होतीं। *अतएव*, पाणिनि ने इन शब्दों को साक्षात् प्रस्तुत कर दिया और उन्हें निपातन के आधार पर ही शुद्ध मान लिया गया।

संक्षेप में, निपातन व्याकरण का वह सिद्धांत है, जिसके अंतर्गत कुछ शब्दों को उनकी व्युत्पत्ति की जटिलता में गए बिना, प्रामाणिक वैयाकरणों द्वारा दिए गए स्वरूप के आधार पर ही शुद्ध मान लिया जाता है।

9/5/25, 10:50 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: युष्मद्युपपदे समानाधिकरणे *स्थानिन्यपि* मध्यमः॥१।४।१०५॥

यदा युष्मच्छब्देन तथा च तिङ्प्रत्ययेन समानपदार्थस्य निर्देशः भवति तदा युष्मच्छब्दस्य उपस्थितौ *अनुपस्थितौ चापि* धातोः मध्यमपुरुषस्यैव तिङ्प्रत्ययाः विधीयन्ते । यथा त्वं पश्यसि। *किं पश्यसि।* त्वं दृश्यसे ।

अपि इति शब्दः स्थानिनि इत्यनेन सह अन्वेति । स्थानिनि अपि इत्युक्ते उपस्थितः अस्ति चेद् अपि इति । अत्र अपि इति शब्दः समुच्चयं दर्शयति । इत्युक्ते *अनुपस्थितः अस्ति चेद् तु अवश्यमेव भवति* परन्तु उपस्थितः अस्ति चेद् अपि भवति इति अत्र अर्थः जायते ।

काशिका   : https://ashtadhyayi.com/kashika/5?scroll=kashika-14105 
लस्य ३.४.७७ : https://ashtadhyayi.com/sutraani/3/4/77 इत्यधिकृत्य सामान्येन तिबादयो विहिताः, तेषामयं पुरुषनियमः क्रियते। युष्मद्युपपदे सति *व्यवहिते चाव्यवहिते* सति समानाधिकरणे समानाभिधेये तुल्यकारके स्थानिनि *प्रयुज्यमानेऽप्यप्रयुज्यमानेऽपि* — पचसि। पचथः। पचथ॥

9/8/25, 10:47 AM - +919*******3318: Here is the explanation given for the peculiar form by Sayana: "धीमहि । ध्यायतेर्लिङि • बहुलं छन्दसि ' इति 'संप्रसारणम् । व्यत्ययेनात्मनेपदम् । यद्वा । ‘धीङ् आधारे । लिङि ‘ बहुलं छन्दसि ' इति विकरणस्य लुक् ।"

He proposes two possible derivations, one with ध्यै चिन्तायाम् dhAtu and other with धीङ् आधारे. Here's the "Paninian prakriyA" you asked for in both the cases.

ध्यै चिन्तायाम्

ध्यै + लिँङ्

ध्यै + महिङ् [बहुलं छन्दसि इति व्यत्ययेनात्पनेपदम्]

ध्यै + सीयुँट् + महि [ लिङस्सीयुट् ३.४.१०२]

ध्या + सीय् + महि [ आदेच उपदेशेऽशिति ६.१.४५]

ध् + इ + आ + सीय् + महि [बहुलं छन्दसि ६.१.३४ इति सम्प्रसारणम्]

ध् + ई + आ + सीय् + महि [हलः ६.४.२ ]

ध् + ई + सीय् + महि [सम्प्रसारणाच्च ६.१.१०८ ]

धी + ईय् + महि [ लिङः सलोपोऽनन्त्यस्य ७.२.७९]

धी + ई + महि [ लोपो व्योर्वलि ६.१.६६]

धी + महि [ अकः सवर्णे दीर्घः ६.१.१०१]

धीमहि

धीङ् आधारे

धी + लिँङ्

धी + महिङ्

धी + श्यन् + महि [ दिवादिभ्यः श्यन् ३.१.६९]

धी + महि [ बहुलं छन्दसि इति विकरणस्य लुक्]

धी + सीयुँट् + महि [ लिङस्सीयुट् ३.४.१०२]

धी + ईय् + महि [ लिङः सलोपोऽनन्त्यस्य ७.२.७९]

धी + ई + महि [ लोपो व्योर्वलि ६.१.६६]

धी + महि [ अकः सवर्णे दीर्घः ६.१.१०१]

धीमहि

9/9/25, 8:29 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Ok, I understood what's happening here. 

देवकी is being treated as a प्रातिपदिकम् and it is becoming देवकिः which isn't quite accurate but still acceptable.

So देवकिनन्दनः can't be made vigraha as देवकी-नन्दनः It's देवकिनन्दनः.

देवकेः नन्दनः इति देवकिनन्दनः।

इदानीं लोकेस्मिन्ननुपमशिखानां पुनरयं
नखानां पांडित्यं प्रकटयतु कस्मिन् मृगपतिः ॥
please can anyone give me the meaning of this shloka?
9/12/25, 2:26 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Elephants whose temples are darkened by the flow of ichor are only heard at the horizon. The she elephants are pitiable. The deer are of no match. Now in this world, where can this king of beasts display the proficiency of its nails of incomparable sharp edge?
[The poet obliquely bemoans his fate of not being able to wrestle with a matching scholar by describing the fate of a lion who does not find any elephant nearby to fight with. This is “anyokti” [अन्योक्ति] or “anyapadesha” [अन्यापदेश], where the poet obliquely refers to some human situation through an analogical happening in nature. We shall come across many such अन्योक्तिs in this work.]

आरोग्यपूर्वं कुशलं वाच्या माता रुमा  च  मे।।4.55.14।।

9/14/25, 6:37 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: No, because different verb forms are implied. 

माता च रुमा च वाच्ये।
माता वाच्या रुमा च वाच्या।
9/14/25, 6:38 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: वाच्या is not a verb form but still said it for simplicity
9/14/25, 6:39 PM - +919*******3318: Is वाच्या not in tritya vibhakti?
9/14/25, 6:40 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: I think it depends on the context. It's a formality to ask for the health of a person before sending your well-being
9/14/25, 6:40 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Nope. First
9/14/25, 6:41 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: आदरणीय माता जी आपका स्वास्थ्य कैसा है। मैं यहाँ कुशल मंगल हूँ। Like that 😅

तत्र अधिकरणं त्रिविधं
व्यापकं, औपश्लेषिकं, वैषयिकं।
 व्यापकं यत्र वस्तु आधारं कार्त्स्न्येन व्याप्य  व्यवतिष्ठते, यथा तिलेषु तैलम्।
औपश्लेषिकं- यत्र आधारस्य अंशं व्याप्य वस्तुस्थिति:। यथा ''कटे तिष्ठति।''

9/19/25, 10:13 AM - +919*******9937: यकारादिप्रत्ययस्य विषयतायां सत्यामेव लभ धातो: नुमागमः।
अर्थात् यकारादि प्रत्यय अभी उपस्थित नही हुआ है, किन्तु उसकी विषयता हो गयी है, साधारण शब्दों में उसके प्रयोग का प्रसंग उपस्थित हो गया है। 
लभ धातु को पोरदुपधात् से यत् प्रत्यय प्राप्त था, अब यकारादि प्रत्यय का प्रसंग प्राप्त होने पर, यकारादि प्रत्यय उपस्थित हो उससे पूर्व ही , नुमागम हो गया, ऐसे में उपधा में अ नहीं रहा, म् आ गया। इसीलिए ऋहलोर्० सूत्र की प्रवृत्ति हुई।
यदि यकारादि प्रत्यय की उपस्थिति होने के बाद नुमागम होता तो यत् प्रत्यय होता ना कि ण्यत्।।

गरबा, रास, और दांडिया - नवरात्र के उत्सवों की व्युत्पत्ति

-कुशाग्र अनिकेत

प्रायः लोकभाषाओं में “नवरात्र” और “नवरात्रि” - दोनों प्रचलित हो गए हैं। संस्कृत व्याकरण के अनुसार “नवरात्र” ही शुद्ध है। यह एक द्विगु-समास है जिसका अर्थ है “नौ रात्रियों का समाहार”। पाणिनीय व्याकरण के अनुसार “रात्रि” से पहले जब संख्या जोड़कर समास बनाया जाता है, तो वहाँ “रात्रि” का रूप परिवर्तित होकर “रात्र” रह जाता है। इसी प्रकार द्विरात्र, त्रिरात्र आदि शब्द भी बनते हैं। “नवरात्र” शब्द का प्रयोग प्रायः सभी ग्रन्थों में मिलता है, “नवरात्रि” का नहीं। किन्तु सामान्य बोलचाल में प्रचलित हो जाने के कारण हिन्दी में “नवरात्रि” भी बहुधा देखने मिलता है।

देश के विभिन्न प्रदेशों में नवरात्र में कुछ विशेष उत्सवों का आयोजन होता है। इनमें गुजरात का “गरबा” आज पूरे विश्व में प्रसिद्ध है। “गरबा” की व्युत्पत्ति संस्कृत के “गर्भ” से मानी गई है। इस आधार पर इस नृत्य के विषय में अनुमान लगाया जा सकता है कि पूर्वकाल में गरबा देवालयों के गर्भ-मण्डप में खेला जाता था। यह भी कहा जा सकता है कि यह नृत्य घर अथवा हवेली के आँगन के केन्द्र में रखे एक विशिष्ट “गर्भ-दीप” के चारों ओर खेला जाता था। इस प्रकार गरबा मन्दिरों में प्रतिमा अथवा घरों में दीपज्योति के रूप में प्रतिष्ठित देवी की आराधना का उत्सव है।

कभी-कभी गरबा के पूर्व “रास” शब्द भी जोड़ा जाता है। रास से सभी परिचित हैं। मूलतः यह गोपालकों का एक नृत्य था जिसका आयोजन रात्रि के समय एक मण्डलाकार रूप में होता था। बाद में श्रीकृष्ण की लीला का अंग बनकर यह मण्डल की परिधि पर स्थित जीवात्माओं के केन्द्र में स्थित परमात्मा के साथ मिलन का प्रतीक बन गया। वर्तमान में प्रचलित गरबा में रास के इस प्राचीन प्रारूप की झलक मिलती है।

गरबा में प्रयोग किए जाने वाले “दांडिया” की उत्पत्ति स्पष्टतया “दण्ड” से हुई है। मध्यकालीन अष्टछाप कवियों ने इसी शब्द से उत्पन्न “दांडी” का वर्णन किया है, जिसका प्रयोग श्रीकृष्ण की लीलाओं में देखा जाता था।

* नवानां रात्रीणां समाहारो नवरात्रम्। अहस्सर्वैकदेशसंख्यातपुण्याच्च रात्रेः (पाणिनीय-सूत्र ५.४.८७)। सङ्ख्यापूर्वं रात्रं क्लीबम्। तत्र वार्त्तिकम्।

*इस ग्रुप में आप संस्कृत भाषा और व्याकरण से संबंधित प्रश्न पूछ सकते हैं। संस्कृत से संबंधित प्रश्नों के अलावा अन्य संदेश भेजने की बिल्कुल मनाही है। नियम का उल्लंघन करने वालों को स्पैम रिपोर्ट कर दिया जायेगा जिससे आपका अकाउंट भी बैन हो सकता है।*

9/23/25, 7:17 PM - ~ Manish kumar left
9/23/25, 7:41 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: आरण्यक ग्रंथों में पर्यावरण-सम्बन्धी तत्वों का उल्लेख आधुनिक पर्यावरणवाद की तरह नहीं, अपितु एक आध्यात्मिक और दार्शनिक दृष्टिकोण से किया गया है। इन ग्रंथों में प्रकृति को ब्रह्म का साकार रूप माना गया है और उसके प्रत्येक तत्व के प्रति गहरी श्रद्धा और सामंजस्य का भाव व्यक्त किया गया है।

आरण्यक ग्रंथ मुख्यतः गद्य (prose) में हैं, इसलिए उनमें पारंपरिक 'श्लोक' (verses) कम मिलते हैं। यहाँ प्रस्तुत पंक्तियाँ महत्वपूर्ण गद्यांश या सूत्रात्मक वाक्य हैं, जो श्लोकों की तरह ही गहन अर्थ रखती हैं।

### आरण्यक ग्रंथों में प्रमुख पर्यावरणीय तत्व एवं संबंधित संस्कृत वाक्य

#### 1. अरण्य (वन) का महत्व (The Importance of the Forest)

आरण्यक ग्रंथों का नाम ही 'अरण्य' अर्थात् वन पर आधारित है। वनों को केवल वृक्षों का समूह नहीं, बल्कि तपोभूमि, साधना और आत्म-ज्ञान की स्थली माना गया है। वन का शांत वातावरण आध्यात्मिक चिंतन के लिए सर्वश्रेष्ठ माना जाता था।

*वैदिक वाक्य:*
> *"अरण्येऽधीयीत"*

*स्रोत:* तैत्तिरीय आरण्यक
*अर्थ:* "(वेद का) अध्ययन अरण्य (वन) में करना चाहिए।"
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* यह पंक्ति वन के शांत और पवित्र वातावरण को ज्ञान-प्राप्ति के लिए अनिवार्य मानती है। यह प्रकृति और शिक्षा के गहरे संबंध को दर्शाती है, जहाँ मनुष्य प्रकृति की गोद में रहकर ही सर्वोच्च ज्ञान प्राप्त कर सकता है।

---

#### 2. पञ्चमहाभूतों की संकल्पना (The Concept of the Five Great Elements)

आरण्यक और उनसे जुड़े उपनिषद् यह स्थापित करते हैं कि सम्पूर्ण ब्रह्मांड और मनुष्य का शरीर पाँच मूल तत्वों- आकाश, वायु, अग्नि, जल और पृथ्वी से बना है। इन तत्वों की शुद्धि और संतुलन पर ही जीवन का संतुलन निर्भर है।

*वैदिक वाक्य:*
> *"तस्माद्वा एतस्मादात्मन आकाशः सम्भूतः। आकाशाद्वायुः। वायोरग्निः। अग्नेरापः। अद्भ्यः पृथिवी।"*

*स्रोत:* तैत्तिरीय उपनिषद् (जो तैत्तिरीय आरण्यक का ही भाग है)
*अर्थ:* "उस परब्रह्म आत्मा से आकाश उत्पन्न हुआ। आकाश से वायु, वायु से अग्नि, अग्नि से जल और जल से पृथ्वी उत्पन्न हुई।"
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* यह सृष्टि की प्रक्रिया को दर्शाता है, जिसमें सभी प्राकृतिक तत्व एक-दूसरे से उत्पन्न और परस्पर जुड़े हुए हैं। यह ब्रह्मांड की पारिस्थितिक एकता (ecological unity) का मूल सिद्धांत है। मनुष्य को यह अहसास कराया जाता है कि वह इसी प्राकृतिक श्रृंखला का एक हिस्सा है।

---

#### 3. सूर्य - प्राण और ऊर्जा का स्रोत (The Sun - The Source of Life-Force and Energy)

सूर्य को केवल एक खगोलीय पिंड नहीं, बल्कि समस्त जीवन का स्रोत, प्राण-शक्ति और साक्षात् ब्रह्म का प्रतीक माना गया है।

*वैदिक वाक्य:*
> *"असौ वा आदित्यो ब्रह्म"*

*स्रोत:* तैत्तिरीय आरण्यक
*अर्थ:* "यह जो आदित्य (सूर्य) है, वही ब्रह्म है।"
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* सूर्य को सर्वोच्च चेतना के रूप में पूजना यह दर्शाता है कि हमारे पूर्वज प्रकृति की ऊर्जा के सबसे बड़े स्रोत के प्रति कितने कृतज्ञ थे। सूर्य की ऊर्जा से ही पृथ्वी पर जीवन संभव है, और यह वाक्य उसी जैविक और आध्यात्मिक सत्य को प्रकट करता है।

---

#### 4. जल की पवित्रता (The Sanctity of Water)

जल को जीवन-तत्त्व और शुद्ध करने वाला माध्यम माना गया है। नदियों और जलाशयों को पवित्र और पूजनीय समझा जाता था।

*वैदिक वाक्य:*
> *"आपो वै सर्वा देवताः"*

*स्रोत:* तैत्तिरीय आरण्यक
*अर्थ:* "जल ही समस्त देवता हैं।"
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* यह सूक्ति जल के महत्व को चरम पर ले जाती है। जब जल को ही सभी देवताओं का रूप मान लिया जाए, तो उसका संरक्षण, उसकी शुद्धि और उसका सम्मान करना एक स्वाभाविक आध्यात्मिक कर्तव्य बन जाता है। यह जल प्रदूषण के विरुद्ध एक सशक्त संदेश है।

---

#### 5. वृक्ष और वनस्पति (Trees and Plants)

वृक्षों और वनस्पतियों को केवल उपयोग की वस्तु नहीं, बल्कि जीवन-दाता और पूजनीय माना गया है। उन्हें अन्न और औषधि का स्रोत मानकर उनके प्रति कृतज्ञता व्यक्त की गई है।

*वैदिक वाक्य:*
> *"अन्नं ब्रह्म"*

*स्रोत:* तैत्तिरीय उपनिषद् (तैत्तिरीय आरण्यक का अंश)
*अर्थ:* "अन्न ही ब्रह्म है।"
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* अन्न वनस्पतियों से ही प्राप्त होता है। अन्न को ब्रह्म कहकर भोजन और उसे उत्पन्न करने वाली वनस्पति-जगत के प्रति सर्वोच्च सम्मान व्यक्त किया गया है। यह भोजन की बर्बादी को रोकने और कृषि तथा वनस्पतियों के संरक्षण का संदेश देता है।

---

#### 6. प्राणी जगत के साथ सह-अस्तित्व (Co-existence with the Animal Kingdom)

आरण्यकों में यह चेतना स्पष्ट है कि मनुष्य इस पृथ्वी पर अकेला नहीं है। अन्य जीव-जंतु भी उसी दैवीय चेतना के अंश हैं।

*वैदिक वाक्य (ऐतरेय आरण्यक से सम्बंधित):*
> *"अग्निर्वाग्भूत्वा मुखं प्राविशद् वायुः प्राणो भूत्वा नासिके प्राविशत्..."*

*स्रोत:* ऐतरेय उपनिषद् (ऐतरेय आरण्यक का भाग)
*अर्थ:* "अग्नि देवता वाणी बनकर मुख में प्रविष्ट हुए, वायु देवता प्राण बनकर नासिका में प्रविष्ट हुए..."
*पर्यावरणीय तत्व:* यह सृष्टि-वर्णन बताता है कि कैसे विभिन्न दैवीय शक्तियाँ केवल मनुष्य में ही नहीं, बल्कि गाय, घोड़े आदि अन्य प्राणियों में भी प्रविष्ट हुईं। इसका गहरा अर्थ यह है कि सभी प्राणियों में एक ही दैवीय चेतना विद्यमान है। यह 'अहिंसा' और सभी जीवों के प्रति करुणा का आधार है।

### निष्कर्ष

आरण्यक ग्रंथों में पर्यावरण की अवधारणा संरक्षण के नियमों के रूप में नहीं, बल्कि एक आध्यात्मिक जीवन-शैली के रूप में प्रस्तुत की गई है। यहाँ प्रकृति को ईश्वर से अलग नहीं, बल्कि उसी का विस्तार माना गया है। जब वन, सूर्य, जल, पृथ्वी, और प्राणी सब ब्रह्म के ही रूप हैं, तो उनका सम्मान और संरक्षण मनुष्य का सर्वोच्च आध्यात्मिक कर्तव्य बन जाता है। यह दृष्टि आज के पर्यावरण संकट के लिए एक गहन और स्थायी समाधान प्रस्तुत करती है।

9/24/25, 8:21 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: _Kṛṣṇa-karṇāmṛta_ (40) describes _Śrī Rādhā’s_ longing to meet _Śrī Kṛṣṇa_:

 *Commentary* : Bhajana-rahasya-vṛtti:
The sakhīs are consoling Śrī Vṛṣabhānu-nandinī, who is suffering in separation from Kṛṣṇa. Suddenly, Śrī Rādhā looks here and there and says, “O sakhī, listen! It is the sound of Kṛṣṇa’s ankle-bells! But why is He not coming before My eyes? Surely that rogue is dallying with some beautiful girl in a nearby kuñja.” While speaking like this, Śrī Rādhā becomes mad, and in that frenzied condition, She sees that Śrī Kṛṣṇa has come. On His body, though, are signs that He has been enjoying with another woman. Upon seeing the marks, Śrī Rādhā becomes indignant (amarṣa ) and, even though Śrī Kṛṣṇa is present before Her, She does not speak to Him but turns Her face away. Śrī Kṛṣṇa then disappears and Rādhā eagerly begins to search for Him here and there.

Restlessness resulting from a delay in seeing and attaining the desired object is called autsukya, ardent desire. When bhāvas overlap, it is known as bhāva-śābalya. When autsukya and asūyā (envy) awaken simultaneously, Śrī Rādhā sometimes criticises Kṛṣṇa, and some times She praises Him. Sometimes She goes into māna, some times She becomes proud and sometimes She praises Him sarcastically.

She says, “O Deva, because You sport with other women, You are called Deva{GL_NOTE::}.” Due to this disrespect, Kṛṣṇa leaves. Repentant, Śrī Rādhā begins to speak out of an eager longing to again enjoy His presence. “O beloved, You are the most dear of My life. Why did You desert Me? Please, grant Me Your darśana.”

Hearing this, Śrī Kṛṣṇa again appears. As soon as Śrī Rādhā sees Him, the sentiments of amarṣa (indignation) and asūyā (envy) arise in Her, and She sarcastically derides Him. “O only friend of the three worlds (bhuvanaika-bandhu), by playing Your enchanting flute, You bring all women under Your control, and for this reason You are the friend of the entire world. You are the friend of all gopīs, therefore You are always near them. Isn’t it Your own fault then, that You do not come to Me?”

Kṛṣṇa again disappears. Not seeing Him, Śrī Rādhā says, “O Kṛṣṇa! O Śyāmasundara! You attract the hearts of the entire universe. After stealing My heart, where have You gone? Please, kindly let Me see You, just once.”

Śrī Kṛṣṇa returns and says, “O Priyā, I was just outside the kuñja. Please, be satisfied with Me.”

Upon hearing Śrī Kṛṣṇa’s entreaty, which is mixed with fickleness, Rādhā says, “O fickle one (capala)! O snake of the cowherd maidens! Please, go away from here, stealer of other men’s wives! I have no need for You. Go back where You came from.”

Kṛṣṇa hears this and pretends to leave. Śrī Vṛṣabhānu-nandinī understands that Her prāṇanātha has gone and offers a supplication. “O only ocean of mercy, I know that I am an offender, but You are the ocean of compassion and Your heart is soft, so please give Me Your darśana. O lover (ramaṇa), You are always sporting with Me, so please come to the kuñja.”

Within Her mind, She understands that Kṛṣṇa has again arrived. Thus absorbed, She holds out Her arms to embrace Him but is unable to do so. Coming to external consciousness, She begins speaking in extreme distress, “O beloved to My eyes (nayanābhirāma )! O You who give joy to the eyes (nayanānanda)! My eyes are very thirsty to gaze upon You. Alas! When will You appear before them?”

9/26/25, 8:52 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: *In English*

*A special note:* Previously, in the present participle forms (*śatṛ* forms) we have seen, the nominative singular ends in 'n' (for example, in *gacchan* - going, *paṭhan* - reading, etc.), right?

For verbs belonging to the 3rd group (*gaṇa*), it does not end in 'n' like this. Instead, it ends in 't'. Among the 3rd group verbs, *dadāti* (he/she gives) is a well-known example. Its present participle form is *dadat* (giving). (The form *'dadan'* is incorrect.)

The words *'dadāti'* and *'yacchati'* have the same meaning (to give). Therefore, it is easier and more common to use *yacchanti* (they give) in place of *dadati*, and to use *yacchan* (giving) in place of *dadat*.

*In Tamil (தமிழில்)*

*ஒரு சிறப்பு குறிப்பு:* இதற்கு முன் நாம் பார்த்த நிகழ்கால வினையெச்ச வடிவங்களில் (śatṛ forms), ஒருமையில் (nominative singular) இறுதியில் 'ந்' என்ற எழுத்து வருவதைப் பார்த்தோம் அல்லவா? (உதாரணமாக: *gacchan* - செல்கின்ற, *paṭhan* - படிக்கின்ற).

ஆனால், மூன்றாம் குழுவைச் சேர்ந்த வினைச்சொற்களுக்கு இவ்வாறு 'ந்' வராது. அதற்குப் பதிலாக, 'த்' என்ற எழுத்து வரும். மூன்றாம் குழு வினைச்சொற்களில் *dadāti* (கொடுக்கிறான்/ள்) என்பது ஒரு பிரபலமான உதாரணம். அதனுடைய நிகழ்கால வினையெச்ச வடிவம் *dadat* (கொடுக்கின்ற) என்பதாகும். (*'dadan'* என்று கூறுவது தவறானது.)

*'dadāti'* மற்றும் *'yacchati'* ஆகிய இரண்டிற்கும் 'கொடு' என்பதே பொருள். எனவே, *dadati* (கொடுக்கிறார்கள்) என்பதற்குப் பதிலாக *yacchanti* என்றும், *dadat* (கொடுக்கின்ற) என்பதற்குப் பதிலாக *yacchan* என்றும் பயன்படுத்துவது எளிமையாகவும் வழக்கத்திலும் உள்ளது.

9/28/25, 8:06 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Is there a Sanskrit composition analogous to the song titled "The Break‑Up Song" (youtu.be/hAB2MptEqWQ) from Ae Dil Hai Mushkil — one that celebrates वियोग (separation/parting), broadly, (not limited to breakups)?
9/28/25, 8:50 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: *वियोगोत्सवः*

*ध्रुवपदम्*

वियोगोऽयं ममोत्सवः। जातो हृदि महान् हर्षः॥

*अन्तराः*

*१.*
गतः स कलहो नित्यम्। गता सा अश्रुधारा च॥
अद्यप्रभृति केवलं सुखम्। जीवनं मे नवं जातम्॥
वियोगोऽयं ममोत्सवः। जातो हृदि महान् हर्षः॥

*२.*
स्वेच्छया कुत्रापि गच्छामि। मित्रैः सह हसामि उच्चैः॥
न कोऽपि रोद्धा न च प्रश्नः। पूर्णं स्वातन्त्र्यम् अद्य मे॥
वियोगोऽयं ममोत्सवः। जातो हृदि महान् हर्षः॥

*३.*
स्वस्ति तेऽस्तु गच्छ दूरम्। भिन्नो मार्गस्तव भिन्नो मम॥
स्मृतिषु त्वं न निवसिष्यसि। नाहं द्रक्ष्यामि पृष्ठतः॥
वियोगोऽयं ममोत्सवः। जातो हृदि महान् हर्षः॥

**

*Translation*

*Title:* The Festival of Separation

*Refrain:*
This separation is my festival. A great joy is born in my heart.

*Verses:*

*1.*
Gone is that constant quarrelling. and gone is that stream of tears.
From this day forth, there is only happiness. my life has become new.
This separation is my festival. A great joy is born in my heart.

*2.*
I go wherever I please. I laugh loudly with my friends.
There is no one to stop me and no questioning. today my freedom is absolute.
This separation is my festival. A great joy is born in my heart.

*3.*
Farewell to you, go far away. Your path is separate, and mine is separate.
You will no longer live in my memories. and I will not look back.
This separation is my festival. A great joy is born in my heart.

*why?*
in some Bollywood film - Satyam Shivam Sundaram bhajan
the lyrics was - Shiva is Satya and Shiva is Sundaram..

but when learning sanskrit.. i got to know all these words are neuter.. shiva cannot be neuter.. 
here neuter shivam means.. auspicious/divine

The meaning of this sentence was 
सत्यम् शिवम् सुन्दरम्  => Truth is Auspicious and Beautiful

---

just wanted to double check my understanding here..? if i got this correct?

9/29/25, 10:42 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: *हाँ, वैदिक संस्कृत की कुछ परंपराओं में, "ष" (ṣa) अक्षर का उच्चारण "ख" (kha) के रूप में किया जाता है। यह अनूठी ध्वन्यात्मक विशेषता मुख्य रूप से शुक्ल यजुर्वेद की माध्यन्दिन शाखा से जुड़ी है, विशेषकर उत्तर भारत में इसके अनुयायियों के मध्य।*

यद्यपि लौकिक संस्कृत में "ष" का मानक उच्चारण एक मूर्धन्य ऊष्म वर्ण ([ʂ]) है, तथापि कुछ वैदिक शाखाओं ने भिन्न उच्चारण परंपराओं को संरक्षित रखा है। "ष" का "ख" में यह परिवर्तन माध्यन्दिन शाखा की पाठ-पद्धति की एक सु-प्रलेखित विशेषता है।

यह विशिष्ट उच्चारण कोई यादृच्छिक अथवा अशुद्ध भिन्नता नहीं है, अपितु इसे इस विशिष्ट शाखा के भीतर पीढ़ियों से चले आ रहे वैदिक मंत्रोच्चार के पारंपरिक नियमों का एक अंग माना जाता है। इन ध्वन्यात्मक नियमों का विस्तार शिक्षा एवं प्रातिशाख्य नाम से ज्ञात प्राचीन ग्रंथों में मिलता है। ये वेदों के सहायक ग्रंथ हैं जो ध्वनि-विज्ञान, उच्चारण, एवं स्वराघात पर केंद्रित हैं।

उदाहरणार्थ, माध्यन्दिन परंपरा के अनुयायियों द्वारा यजुर्वेदीय पुरुष सूक्त के पाठ के समय, "पुरुष" शब्द का उच्चारण 'पुरुख' जैसा सुनाई देता है। यह अन्य वैदिक शाखाओं, जैसे कि ऋग्वेद का अनुसरण करने वाली शाखाओं, के पाठ के विपरीत है, जो "ष" की मानक ध्वनि को बनाए रखती हैं।

यद्यपि यह प्रथा सर्वमान्य है, तथापि किसी विशिष्ट शिक्षा अथवा प्रातिशाख्य ग्रंथ में उस सटीक श्लोक को इंगित करना कठिन हो सकता है जो स्पष्ट रूप से इस परिवर्तन का विधान करता हो, क्योंकि इस ज्ञान का अधिकांश भाग मौखिक रूप से प्रसारित हुआ था।

यह ध्यान रखना महत्वपूर्ण है कि यह एक विशेष वैदिक शाखा का विशिष्ट लक्षण है, न कि वैदिक संस्कृत के सभी रूपों में लागू होने वाला कोई सार्वभौमिक नियम। इस प्रकार की भिन्नताओं का अस्तित्व उन मौखिक परंपराओं की समृद्ध विविधता को उजागर करता है, जिन्होंने सहस्राब्दियों से वैदिक संहिताओं को संरक्षित रखा है।

9/29/25, 10:57 AM - +919*******6427: *ऋटुरषा मूर्धन्याः*
ऋ ऋ ऋ३, (दु=) ट ठ ड ढ ण, र और ष का उच्चारण मूर्द्धा स्थान अर्थात् तालु के ऊपर से करना चाहिये। जैसी क्रिया ट के उच्चारण में की जाती है, वैसे ही *ष के उच्चारण* में करनी उचित है।

*इचुयशास्तालव्याः*

9/29/25, 11:32 AM - मोहित डोकानिया: *शुक्ल यजुर्वेद की माध्यन्दिन शाखा के अनुयायियों द्वारा संस्कृत व्यंजन 'ष' का 'ख' के रूप में विशिष्ट उच्चारण एक ऐसी प्रथा है, जिसका मूल उनके विशिष्ट शिक्षा-ग्रन्थों में निहित है।* यह परम्परा, जो इस शाखा के उत्तर भारतीय पण्डितों में विशेष रूप से प्रचलित है, कोई मनमाना परिवर्तन नहीं, अपितु उनके वैदिक पाठ की एक नियमबद्ध विशेषता है।

इस अद्वितीय ध्वनि-नियम का मुख्य शास्त्रीय प्रमाण **लघुमाध्यन्दिन शिक्षा** नामक ग्रन्थ में मिलता है। इस ग्रन्थ का प्रथम श्लोक ही इस उच्चारण-सम्बन्धी निर्देश को स्पष्ट रूप से उल्लिखित करता है:

> **षकारस्य खकारः स्याट्टुकयोगे तु नो भवेत् ।**

इस श्लोक का अर्थ है: "**'ष' अक्षर का उच्चारण 'ख' होता है, परन्तु जब यह 'ट' के साथ संयुक्त होता है, तब ऐसा नहीं होता।**" यह संक्षिप्त नियम माध्यन्दिन परम्परा के भीतर मानक उच्चारण स्थापित करता है और साथ ही इस नियम का एक अपवाद भी निर्दिष्ट करता है।

उदाहरण के लिए, वेदों में पाए जाने वाले पुरुष सूक्त का पाठ करते समय माध्यन्दिन शाखा के अनुयायी "पुरुषः" (पुरुष्ः) शब्द का उच्चारण "पुरुखः" करते हैं। अपने शिक्षा ग्रन्थ के प्रति यह निष्ठा, उनकी विशिष्ट वाचन परम्परा के संरक्षण की प्रतिबद्धता की पहचान है।

शिक्षा ग्रन्थ छह वेदांगों (वैदिक अध्ययन के सहायक शास्त्रों) में से एक हैं और वे वैदिक भाषा के शुद्ध उच्चारण, स्वर तथा ध्वनि-विज्ञान पर केन्द्रित हैं। प्रत्येक वैदिक शाखा के अपने ग्रन्थ होते हैं जो उनकी विशेष मौखिक परम्पराओं को संहिताबद्ध करते हैं। शुक्ल यजुर्वेद के लिए *याज्ञवल्क्य शिक्षा* एक आधारभूत ग्रन्थ है, और लघुमाध्यन्दिन शिक्षा, माध्यन्दिन संस्करण के लिए विशिष्ट नियम प्रदान करती है।

अतः, 'ष' का 'ख' के रूप में उच्चारण माध्यन्दिन शाखा की मौखिक परम्परा का एक ऐतिहासिक और शास्त्रीय रूप से स्वीकृत पक्ष है। यह अध्ययन की विभिन्न शाखाओं के भीतर वैदिक पाठ की ध्वन्यात्मक अखंडता को बनाए रखने के लिए दिए गए सूक्ष्म ध्यान को प्रदर्शित करता है।

10/1/25, 1:22 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Asked by @⁨~Kannan⁩
10/1/25, 1:22 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: *Gaurīvallabhaka Mārakāla Kūṭaviṣāśana* is an epithet for the Hindu deity Lord Shiva.

*Meaning:* "The beloved of Gauri (Parvati), the destroyer of Māra (Kama, the god of desire), and the consumer of the deadly Kūṭa poison."

*Explanation:* This single phrase praises three key aspects of Shiva:
1.  His role as a loving husband to Parvati.
2.  His supreme ascetic power in conquering desire by incinerating Kama Deva.
3.  His compassion in saving the universe by drinking the Halahala poison.

*தமிழ் மொழிபெயர்ப்பு (Tamil Translation):*

*கௌரீவல்லபக மரகால கூடவிஷாசன* என்பது இந்து கடவுளான சிவபெருமானைக் குறிக்கும் ஒரு பட்டம்.

*பொருள்:* "கௌரியின் (பார்வதியின்) அன்பிற்குரியவர், மாரனை (மன்மதனை) அழித்தவர், மற்றும் கொடிய கூட விஷத்தை உட்கொண்டவர்."

*விளக்கம்:* இந்த ஒற்றைச் சொற்றொடர் சிவனின் மூன்று முக்கிய அம்சங்களைப் புகழ்கிறது:
1.  பார்வதியின் அன்பான கணவராக அவரது பங்கு.
2.  மன்மதனை எரித்து, ஆசையை வென்ற அவரது உச்சநிலை துறவற சக்தி.
3.  ஆலகால விஷத்தை அருந்தி பிரபஞ்சத்தைக் காத்த அவரது கருணை.

10/1/25, 3:07 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: The matra for ए is े and for ऐ is ै — these are already distinct matras, not written as separate full letters when attached to consonants: के (क + े), कै (क + ै). So ए and ऐ are not lacking matras; they use े and ै respectively.

10/4/25, 5:52 PM - +919*******5025: How is अभञ्जितम् derived?
10/4/25, 5:53 PM - +919*******1750 joined using a group link.
10/4/25, 5:54 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: भञ्जित
भञ्ज् + णिचि + क्त

 *भञ्ज्* आमर्दने
→ भन्जोँ [ भूवादयो धातवः १.३.१]
→ भन्ज् + णिच् [ हेतुमति च ३.१.२६]
→ भन्ज् + इ + क्त [ निष्ठा ३.२.१०२]
→ भन्ज् + इ + इट् + त [ आर्धधातुकस्येड् वलादेः ७.२.३५]
→ भन्ज् + इ + त [ निष्ठायां सेटि ६.४.५२]
→ भंज् + इ + त [ नश्चापदान्तस्य झलि ८.३.२४]
→ भञ्ज् + इ + त [ अनुस्वारस्य ययि परसवर्णः ८.४.५८]
→ भञ्जित

10/23/25, 7:21 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: I didn't understand your question but prakriya might help.

 *षष्* 
→ षष् + आम् [ षष्ठीबहुवचनविवक्षायां स्वौजसमौट्छष्टाभ्याम्भिस्ङेभ्याम्भ्यस्ङसिभ्याम्भ्यस्ङसोसाङ्ङ्योस्सुप् ४.१.२ इति आम्-प्रत्ययः । सुप्तिङन्तं पदम् १.४.१४ इति पदसंज्ञा ।]
→ षष् + आम् [ षकारान्त-संख्यावाचकशब्दस्य ष्णान्ता षट् १.१.२४ इति षट्-संज्ञा ।]
→ षष् + नुट् आम् [ षट्-संज्ञकात् परस्य आम्-प्रत्ययस्य षट्चतुर्भ्यश्च ७.१.५५ इति नुडागमः । टित्त्वात् आद्यन्तौ टकितौ १.१.४६ इति आद्यवयवः ।]
→ षष् + न् आम् [ हलन्त्यम् १.३.३ इति अन्तिम-टकारस्य इत्संज्ञा । तस्य लोपः १.३.९ इति लोपः । नकारोत्तरः उकारः उच्चारणार्थः, अतः सोऽपि लुप्यते ।]
→ षष् + नाम् [ स्वादिष्वसर्वनामस्थाने १.४.१७ इति प्रकृतेः पदसंज्ञा ।]
→ षड् + नाम् [ झलां जशोऽन्ते ८.२.३९ इति पदान्तजश्त्वम् ।]
→ षड्नाम्
→ षड्णाम् [ डकारात् परस्य नकारस्य ष्टुना ष्टुः ८.४.४० इति ष्टुत्वे णकारः ।]
→ षण्णाम् [ यर्-वर्णस्य अनुनासिकवर्णे परे यरोऽनुनासिके प्रत्यये भाषायां नित्यवचनं कर्तव्यम् इति नित्यम् अनुनासिकादेशः । डकारस्य अनुनासिकत्वे णकारः ।।]

10/23/25, 7:39 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: Idk. See if this helps ↓

The crucial steps in your *prakriyā* are:

1.  *षड् + नाम्*: This is the state after *jaśtva* sandhi has been applied by *jhala‌m‌ jaśo'nte* (8.2.39).
2.  *षड्णाम्*: This is the result of applying *stoḥ ṣṭunā ṣṭuḥ* (8.4.41), which changes the dental nasal 'न्' to the retroflex nasal 'ण्' due to its contact with the retroflex 'ड्'.

However, this application of *stoḥ ṣṭunā ṣṭuḥ* is immediately prohibited by the next sutra, *"न पदान्ताट्टोरनाम्" (8.4.42)*. This sutra states that *ṣṭutva* (the transformation from *stoḥ ṣṭunā ṣṭuḥ*) does *not* occur when a dental consonant follows a retroflex consonant of the ट-series (ट्, ठ्, ड्, ढ्, ण्) that is at the end of a word (*padānta*).

The sutra has two parts:
*   *न पदान्ताट्टोः*: The general prohibition (*utsarga*).
*   *अनाम्*: The exception to the prohibition (*apavāda*). It specifies that this prohibition does *not* apply if the following word is "नाम्" (or related forms).

*Answering Your Question*

If the *apavāda* part ("अनाम्") were not there, the sutra would simply be "न पदान्ताट्टोः". In that scenario, the derivation would be blocked.

Here is what would happen:

1.  We reach the stage *षड् + नाम्*.
2.  The rule *stoḥ ṣṭunā ṣṭuḥ* attempts to change 'न्' to 'ण्'.
3.  The general prohibition *na padāntāṭṭoḥ* (without the "अनाम्" exception) would immediately block it, because the 'न्' follows a *padānta* 'ड्' (which belongs to the ट-series).
4.  The *ṣṭutva* would fail to apply.
5.  The next rule, *yaro'nunāsike'nunāsiko vā*, would then apply to "षड् + नाम्". This would change the 'ड्' to its corresponding nasal, 'ण्'.

The resulting incorrect form would be *ण्णाम्* (*ṇṇām*), which is phonetically and grammatically incorrect. The preceding prohibition would prevent the initial and correct transformation of 'न्' to 'ण्'.

Therefore, the incorrect sandhi formed by the over-application of the prohibition (if the exception were absent) would leave the 'न्' as it is, leading to an impossible intermediate stage that results in a wrong final form. The crucial role of the exception "अनाम्" is to "turn off" the prohibition, allowing *stoḥ ṣṭunā ṣṭuḥ* to correctly produce *षड्णाम्*, which then proceeds to become the final, correct form *षण्णाम्*.

10/23/25, 7:56 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: This beautiful verse uses a series of metaphors to describe the appearance of Lord Krishna, depicted as a dark cloud, for the purpose of alleviating suffering.

Here is a word-by-word meaning and a simple explanation of the verse:

> Word-by-Word Meaning

*   *वासोवल्ली (vāsovallī)*: ```वासस्``` (vāsas) means clothing + ```वल्ली``` (vallī) means a creeper or a vine. Together, it means "a creeper-like garment," referring to the yellow dhoti Krishna wears, which clings to him like a vine.
*   *विद्युन्माला (vidyunmālā)*: ```विद्युत्``` (vidyut) means lightning + ```माला``` (mālā) means a garland. Together, it means "a garland of lightning," symbolizing a shining necklace or the brilliance of his ornaments against his dark skin.
*   *बर्हश्रेणी (barhaśreṇī)*: ```बर्ह``` (barha) means a peacock feather + ```श्रेणी``` (śreṇī) means a line or a row. Together, it means "a row of peacock feathers," referring to the peacock feather adorning Krishna's crown.
*   *शाक्रश्चापः (śākraścāpaḥ)*: This is a sandhi of ```शाक्रः``` (śākraḥ) meaning "belonging to Indra" + ```चापः``` (cāpaḥ) meaning "a bow." Together, it means "Indra's bow," which is a rainbow (```इन्द्रधनुष```). This symbolizes Krishna's multi-colored vanamālā (forest garland) or his beautiful, arching eyebrows.
*   *यस्मिन् (yasmin)*: "In which" or "on whom." This refers to the dark cloud, which is Krishna.
*   *आस्तां (āstāṃ)*: "Are present" or "exist."
*   *तापोच्छित्त्यै (tāpocchittyai)*: ```ताप``` (tāpa) means heat, sorrow, or suffering + ```उच्छित्त्यै``` (ucchittyai) means "for the removal/destruction of." Together, it means "for the purpose of removing suffering."
*   *गोमध्यस्थः (gomadhyasthaḥ)*: ```गो``` (go) has multiple meanings including "cows," "senses," or "the earth" + ```मध्य``` (madhya) means "in the middle of" + ```स्थः``` (sthaḥ) means "situated." Together, it means "standing in the midst of the cows."
*   *कृष्णांभोदः (kṛṣṇāmbhadaḥ)*: ```कृष्ण``` (kṛṣṇa) means black or dark + ```अंभोदः``` (ambhodhaḥ) means "a cloud" (literally, "water-giver"). Together, it means "the dark cloud," which is a direct metaphor for Lord Krishna himself, who has a dark complexion like a raincloud.

### Simple Explanation

The verse paints a poetic picture of Lord Krishna by comparing him to a life-giving dark raincloud.

*"The dark cloud (Lord Krishna), standing amidst the cows, is there to end all suffering. On this cloud, the creeper-like garment is the lightning, the row of peacock feathers is a garland of lightning, and his beautiful forest garland (or eyebrows) is the rainbow of Indra."*

In simpler terms, just as a dark raincloud comes to cool the earth and end the misery of a scorching drought, Lord Krishna appears among his devotees (the cows) to relieve them of their worldly sufferings. His yellow clothes are like flashes of lightning against his dark body, his peacock feather shines brilliantly, and his multi-colored garland is like a rainbow appearing with the cloud.

10/26/25, 3:31 PM - +919*******3745: क्या संस्कृत व्याकरण में ऐसे कोई सूत्र हैं क्या जो धातुओं को सेट्, वेट् और अनिट् में विभाजित करते हैं ?
10/26/25, 4:02 PM - मोहित डोकानिया: हाँ, संस्कृत व्याकरण में धातुओं को सेट्, वेट् और अनिट् में विभाजित करने के लिए स्पष्ट नियम और सूत्र हैं। यह विभाजन इस पर आधारित है कि कोई धातु आर्धधातुक प्रत्यय से पहले 'इट्' (इ) का आगम लेती है या नहीं।

यह वर्गीकरण किसी एक सूत्र में नहीं बल्कि सूत्रों की एक व्यवस्था पर आधारित है, जिसमें एक मुख्य नियम और उसके कई अपवाद (निषेध और विकल्प) हैं।

*सेट्, वेट् और अनिट् का अर्थ:*
*   *सेट् (स + इट्):* "इट् के साथ"। ये वे धातुएँ हैं जो आर्धधातुक प्रत्यय से पहले 'इट्' (इ) का आगम अनिवार्य रूप से लेती हैं। उदाहरण के लिए, 'पठ्' धातु सेट् है, इसलिए 'क्त्वा' प्रत्यय लगने पर 'पठित्वा' रूप बनता है।
*   *अनिट् (अन् + इट्):* "इट् के बिना"। ये वे धातुएँ हैं जो 'इट्' का आगम कभी नहीं लेती हैं। उदाहरण के लिए, 'गम्' धातु अनिट् है, इसलिए 'क्त्वा' प्रत्यय लगने पर 'गत्वा' रूप बनता है, 'गमित्वा' नहीं।
*   *वेट् (वा + इट्):* "विकल्प से इट्"। ये वे धातुएँ हैं जो विकल्प से 'इट्' का आगम लेती हैं, यानी कभी लेती हैं और कभी नहीं।

*प्रमुख सूत्र और नियम:*

1.  *मुख्य विधि-सूत्र:* पाणिनि की अष्टाध्यायी का सूत्र *"आर्धधातुकस्येड् वलादेः" (अष्टाध्यायी 7.2.35)* यह एक सामान्य नियम स्थापित करता है। इसका अर्थ है कि 'वल्' प्रत्याहार के वर्ण (य को छोड़कर अन्य सभी व्यंजन) से शुरू होने वाले आर्धधातुक प्रत्यय को 'इट्' का आगम होता है। इस नियम के अनुसार, सैद्धांतिक रूप से सभी धातुओं को 'इट्' का आगम होना चाहिए।

2.  *मुख्य निषेध-सूत्र (अनिट् धातुओं के लिए):*
    *   *"एकाच उपदेशेऽनुदात्तात्" (अष्टाध्यायी 7.2.10):* यह 'इट्' आगम का निषेध करने वाला एक बहुत महत्वपूर्ण सूत्र है। इसके अनुसार, जो धातुएँ उपदेश (मूल पाठ, यानी धातुपाठ) में एकाच् (एक स्वर वाली) हों और अनुदात्त (स्वर-चिह्न रहित) हों, उनसे परे 'इट्' का आगम नहीं होता। अधिकांश अनिट् धातुएँ इसी सूत्र के अंतर्गत आती हैं।
    *   *"श्र्युकः क्किति" (अष्टाध्यायी 7.2.11):* यह सूत्र कहता है कि 'श्रि' धातु और 'उक्' (उ, ऊ, ऋ, ॠ) में समाप्त होने वाली धातुओं के बाद 'क्' या 'ङ्' इत्संज्ञक प्रत्यय होने पर 'इट्' का आगम नहीं होता।

3.  *विकल्प-सूत्र (वेट् धातुओं के लिए):*
    *   *"स्वरतिसूतिसूयतिधूञूदितो वा" (अष्टाध्यायी 7.2.44):* यह सूत्र कुछ विशिष्ट धातुओं जैसे 'स्वृ', 'सू', 'धू' आदि को वेट् के रूप में परिभाषित करता है, जिसका अर्थ है कि वे विकल्प से 'इट्' का आगम लेती हैं।
    *   *"ॠदन्त" धातुएँ:* कुछ ऋकारान्त धातुएँ भी वेट् होती हैं।

इस प्रकार, किसी धातु का सेट्, वेट् या अनिट् होना पाणिनि के व्याकरण में एक विस्तृत और जटिल विषय है, जो मुख्य सूत्र और उसके बाद आने वाले अनेक निषेध और वैकल्पिक सूत्रों के परस्पर अनुप्रयोग से निर्धारित होता है। धातुपाठ में धातुओं का पाठ करते समय उनके उदात्त या अनुदात्त स्वरूप के आधार पर उनके सेट् या अनिट् होने का निर्णय किया जाता है।
